revision.text:
'''Senjafuda''' (千社札) are stickers or scraps of paper posted on the gates of shrines in Japan by pilgrims.
{{Japan-stub}}

[[Image:Gifu-kegonji0059.JPG|right|thumb|300px|''Senjafuda'' pasted on a temple gate in [[Gifu]].]]
'''Senjafuda''' (千社札) are stickers or scraps of paper posted on the gates of shrines in Japan by pilgrims.
{{Japan-stub}}
[[ja:千社札]]

[[Image:Gifu-kegonji0059.JPG|right|thumb|300px|''Senjafuda'' pasted on a shrine gate in [[Gifu]].]]
'''Senjafuda''' (千社札, literally "thousand shrine tags") are stickers or scraps of paper posted on the gates of shrines and Buddhist temples in [[Japan]] by pilgrims. The stickers bear the name of the worshipper, and can be purchased pre-printed with common names at temples and shrines throughout Japan, as well as at stationery stores and video game centres. ''Senjafuda'' were originally made from sheets of copper, but have been made of paper since the [[Edo period]].
Since ''senjafuda'' are now sold as stickers (and no longer require pasting), they are also placed in books and on personal items for identification and decoration.
{{Japan-stub}}
[[ja:千社札]]

[[Image:Gifu-kegonji0059.JPG|right|thumb|300px|''Senjafuda'' pasted on a shrine gate in [[Gifu]].]]
'''Senjafuda''' (千社札, literally "thousand shrine tags") are stickers or scraps of paper posted on the gates of shrines and Buddhist temples in [[Japan]] by pilgrims. The stickers bear the name of the worshipper, and can be purchased pre-printed with common names at temples and shrines throughout Japan, as well as at stationery stores and video game centres. ''Senjafuda'' were originally made from sheets of copper, but have been made of paper since the [[Edo period]].
Since ''senjafuda'' are now sold as stickers (and no longer require pasting), they are also placed in books and on personal items for identification and decoration.
''Senjafuda'' are usually printed in black ink on white paper, but are also available in various colours and styles. [[Edomoji]], or Edo-period lettering styles, are the most common.
{{Japan-stub}}
[[ja:千社札]]

[[Image:Gifu-kegonji0059.JPG|right|thumb|300px|''Senjafuda'' pasted on a shrine gate in [[Gifu]].]]
'''Senjafuda''' (千社札, literally "thousand shrine tags") are stickers or scraps of paper posted on the gates of [[shrines]] and ppBuddhist temple]]s in [[Japan]]. The stickers bear the name of the worshipper, and can be purchased pre-printed with common names at temples and shrines throughout Japan, as well as at stationery stores and video game centres. ''Senjafuda'' were originally made from sheets of copper, but have been made of paper since the [[Edo period]].
Since ''senjafuda'' are now sold as stickers (and no longer require pasting), they are also placed in books and on personal items for identification and decoration.
''Senjafuda'' are usually printed in black ink on white paper, but are also available in various colours and styles. [[Edomoji]], or Edo-period lettering styles, are the most common.
{{Japan-stub}}
[[ja:千社札]]

[[Image:Gifu-kegonji0059.JPG|right|thumb|300px|''Senjafuda'' pasted on a shrine gate in [[Gifu]].]]

''Senjafuda'' were originally made from sheets of copper, but have been made of paper since the [[Edo period]].
Since ''senjafuda'' are now sold as stickers (and no longer require pasting), they are also placed in books and on personal items for identification and decoration.
''Senjafuda'' are usually printed in black ink on white paper, but are also available in various colours and styles. [[Edomoji]], or Edo-period lettering styles, are the most common.
{{Japan-stub}}
[[ja:千社札]]

Since ''senjafuda'' are now sold as stickers (and no longer require pasting), they are also placed in books and on personal items for identification and decoration.

{{Japan-stub}}
[[ja:千社札]]

[[Image:Gifu-kegonji0059.JPG|right|thumb|300px|''Senjafuda'' pasted on a shrine gate in [[Gifu]].]]
'''Senjafuda''' (千社札, literally "thousand shrine tags") are stickers or scraps of paper posted on the gates of [[Shinto shrine|shrines]] and [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] in [[Japan]]. The stickers bear the name of the worshipper, and can be purchased pre-printed with common names at temples and shrines throughout Japan, as well as at stationery stores and video game centres. ''Senjafuda'' were originally made from sheets of copper, but have been made of paper since the [[Edo period]].
Since ''senjafuda'' are now sold as stickers (and no longer require pasting), they are also placed in books and on personal items for identification and decoration.
''Senjafuda'' are usually printed in black ink on white paper, but are also available in various colours and styles. [[Edomoji]], or Edo-period lettering styles, are the most common.
{{Japan-stub}}
[[ja:千社札]]

[[Image:Gifu-kegonji0059.JPG|right|thumb|300px|''Senjafuda'' pasted on a shrine gate in [[Gifu]].]]
'''Senjafuda''' (千社札, literally "thousand shrine tags") are stickers or scraps of paper posted on the gates of [[Shinto shrine|shrines]] and [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] in [[Japan]]. The stickers bear the name of the worshipper, and can be purchased pre-printed with common names at temples and shrines throughout Japan, as well as at stationery stores and video game centres. ''Senjafuda'' were originally made from sheets of copper, but have been made of paper since the [[Edo period]].
== Summary ==
A single senjafuda measures 1.6[[Cun_%28unit%29|Sun]] (58mm) in width and 4.8 Sun (173mm) in height. This gives the senjafuda a ratio of 1:3. A frame is drawn inside this space which contains the lettering or pictures. In 1887 a measurement for this frame was also established as 48mm wide and 144mm tall.
Ordinarily, the designs were used to commemorate a visit to a temple or shrine and printed with simple monochromatic schemes, but eventually aesthetic sense gave way to colorful variations and designs. In the pleasure quarters of [[Kyoto]], colorful designs were employed on senjafuda and used in place of traditional business cards. This variation is called "hana-meishi" which roughly translated to "flower business card." Today, the "business card" use of senjafuda is the most common.
Senjafuda were primarily printed with [[Edomoji]], or Edo-period lettering styles, and pressed with the same traditional wooden boards used to produce [[ukiyo-e]] prints.
Stickers on shrines are often pasted in very obvious, easily seen locations, but a variation on this practice is to purposely obscure the location of the senjafuda in order to protect it from exposure to wind and rain and thus prolong its presence.
== In Recent years ==
''Senjafuda'' are also sold as stickers which do not require separate paste. As stickers they are also placed in books and on personal items for identification and decoration.
{{Japan-stub}}
[[ja:千社札]]

[[Image:Gifu-kegonji0059.JPG|right|thumb|300px|''Senjafuda'' pasted on a shrine gate in [[Gifu]].]]
'''Senjafuda''' (千社札, literally "thousand shrine tags") are stickers or scraps of paper posted on the gates of [[Shinto shrine|shrines]] and [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] in [[Japan]]. The stickers bear the name of the worshipper, and can be purchased pre-printed with common names at temples and shrines throughout Japan, as well as at stationery stores and video game centres. ''Senjafuda'' were originally made from sheets of copper, but have been made of paper since the [[Edo period]].
== Summary ==
A single senjafuda measures 1.6[[Cun_%28unit%29|Sun]] (58mm) in width and 4.8 Sun (173mm) in height. This gives the senjafuda a ratio of 1:3. A frame is drawn inside this space which contains the lettering or pictures. In 1887 a measurement for this frame was also established as 48mm wide and 144mm tall.
Ordinarily, the designs were used to commemorate a visit to a temple or shrine and printed with simple monochromatic schemes, but eventually aesthetic sense gave way to colorful variations and designs. In the pleasure quarters of [[Kyoto]], colorful designs were employed on senjafuda and used in place of traditional business cards. This variation is called "hana-meishi" which roughly translated to "flower business card." Today, the "business card" use of senjafuda is the most common.
Senjafuda were primarily printed with [[Edomoji]], or Edo-period lettering styles, and pressed with the same traditional wooden boards used to produce [[ukiyo-e]] prints.
Stickers on shrines are often pasted in very obvious, easily seen locations, but a variation on this practice is to purposely obscure the location of the senjafuda in order to protect it from exposure to wind and rain and thus prolong its presence.
== In Recent years ==
''Senjafuda'' are also sold as stickers which do not require separate paste. As stickers they are also placed in books and on personal items for identification and decoration. A common criticism of the sticker version of senjafuda is that they are more difficult to peel off than their original pasted ancestors and thus can disfigure the underlying buildings when removed.
{{Japan-stub}}
[[ja:千社札]]

{{Unreferenced|date=November 2008}}

revision.comment: I added the history, construction and famous figures sections based on research I have done in Japanese history.
revision.text:
{{Unreferenced|date=November 2008}}
[[Image:Gifu-kegonji0059.JPG|right|thumb|300px|''Senjafuda'' pasted on a shrine gate in [[Gifu]].]]
'''Senjafuda''' (千社札, literally "thousand shrine tags") are stickers or scraps of paper posted on the gates of [[Shinto shrine|shrines]] and [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] in [[Japan]]. The stickers bear the name of the worshipper, and can be purchased pre-printed with common names at temples and shrines throughout Japan, as well as at stationery stores and video game centres. ''Senjafuda'' were originally made from wooden slats, but have been made of paper since the [[Edo period]].
== Summary ==
A single senjafuda measures 1.6[[Cun_%28unit%29|Sun]] (58mm) in width and 4.8 Sun (173mm) in height. This gives the senjafuda a ratio of 1:3. A frame is drawn inside this space which contains the lettering or pictures. In 1887 a measurement for this frame was also established as 48mm wide and 144mm tall.
Ordinarily, the designs were used to commemorate a visit to a temple or shrine and printed with simple monochromatic schemes, but eventually aesthetic sense gave way to colorful variations and designs. In the pleasure quarters of [[Kyoto]], colorful designs were employed on senjafuda and used in place of traditional business cards. This variation is called "hana-meishi" which roughly translated to "flower business card." Today, the "business card" use of senjafuda is the most common.
Senjafuda were primarily printed with [[Edomoji]], or Edo-period lettering styles, and pressed with the same traditional wooden boards used to produce [[ukiyo-e]] prints.
Stickers on shrines are often pasted in very obvious, easily seen locations, but a variation on this practice is to purposely obscure the location of the senjafuda in order to protect it from exposure to wind and rain and thus prolong its presence.
== History == Senjafuda were first produced in the Heian period 794 to 1185 (平安時代 Heian jidai?) when shrine worshipers made pilgrimages to visits to many shrines and worship the Buddhist goddess of mercy, Kannon. They were not originally made of paper, they were first made from wooden slats that were hung from the gates of Kannon Temples by nails made of bamboo. The slats were carved out with the visitors’ name, area of origin and often included a prayer for a good life and afterlife. There are two styles of Senjafuda, the older style, daimei nosatsu and kokan nosatsu the new style. Daimei nosatsu are basic black ink on white paper. The ink used is called sumi and it is so strong that after the printed senjafuda was placed on the shrine or temple gate, years later when the paper peeled away, the ink remained. Which is why many shrine Kannushi (神主?) or shinshoku (神職?) do not like the use of senjafuda? Add to the fact that younger senjafuda practitioners now days, do not follow tradition and pray nor buy a stamp from the shrine before applying their senjafuda.
The later style senjafuda are called kokan nosatsu and they originated around Japans Edo period, 1603 to 1868 (江戸時代 Edo jidai?). They came about because during the beginning of the Edo period, the pilgrimages to shrines gained popularity and started what is known as senjamode which literally means a thousand shrine visits for good luck. Kokan nosatsu senjafuda are a lot more colorful and have rich patterns and designs. They were used as novelty items and more like trading cards or the business cards of today. Like most all things during the Edo period, they were regulated. Depending on your class and order in society, they could not contain too many colors if you were not upper class. Collectors who enjoyed the many designs and colors of senjafuda would meet to exchange with one another. First the meetings took place at private homes and then later were arranged for public places like restaurants and expensive tea houses. According to Vanishing Japan, by Elizabeth Kiritani, “the oldest surviving invitation card to one of these meetings dates back to 1799.” Of course the government found out about the meetings and enforced law upon them. This did not stop the meetings from taking place, and they continue to this day, collectors and aficionados alike meet to share and trade their own designs as well as admire others.
== Construction ==
Senjafuda used to be made from rice paper with ink called sumi. As for the paste, they used a rice mush. The pilgrims used to carry walking staffs for their long journeys, which doubled as an applicator for senjafuda. “The walking staff which doubles as a collapsible rod some of which are said to be up to 8 meters in length provides the means to paste senjafuda in hard to reach places. According to Vanishing Japan, by Elizabeth Kiritani,” They applied the paste with something called meotobake, two brushes about 30 degrees apart with a clip on the other side of the brushes. This is how they were able to paste the senjafuda in out of reach areas, leaving others to wonder exactly how they got up there.
Now senjafuda are made from printed paper and are rarely made traditional by wood block prints or ukiyo-e. They do make wooden slat senjafuda which are worn as a necklace or used for key chain and cell phone ordainments. The ones made from paper are pre-printed with common names and there are also machines that you can make custom senjafuda with adhesive backings.
== Famous Figures == Some famous producers of senjafuda are Hiroshige, Eisen, Kunisada and Kuniyoshi. They mainly produce senjafuda for collectors because the process of ukiyoe printing is very expensive. Senrei Sekioka is Japans expert in Senjafuda design and trade.
== In recent years ==
''Senjafuda'' are also sold as stickers which do not require separate paste. As stickers they are also placed in books and on personal items for identification and decoration. A common criticism of the sticker version of senjafuda is that they are more difficult to peel off than their original pasted ancestors and thus can disfigure the underlying buildings when removed.
{{Japan-culture-stub}}
{{Uncategorized|date=June 2009}}
[[ja:千社札]]

{{Unreferenced|date=November 2008}}

''Senjafuda'' were originally made from wooden slats, but have been made of paper since the [[Edo period]].
== Summary ==
A single senjafuda measures 1.6[[Cun_%28unit%29|Sun]] (58mm) in width and 4.8 Sun (173mm) in height. This gives the senjafuda a ratio of 1:3. A frame is drawn inside this space which contains the lettering or pictures. In 1887 a measurement for this frame was also established as 48mm wide and 144mm tall.
Ordinarily, the designs were used to commemorate a visit to a temple or shrine and printed with simple monochromatic schemes, but eventually aesthetic sense gave way to colorful variations and designs. In the pleasure quarters of [[Kyoto]], colorful designs were employed on senjafuda and used in place of traditional business cards. This variation is called "hana-meishi" which roughly translated to "flower business card." Today, the "business card" use of senjafuda is the most common.
Senjafuda were primarily printed with [[Edomoji]], or Edo-period lettering styles, and pressed with the same traditional wooden boards used to produce [[ukiyo-e]] prints.
Stickers on shrines are often pasted in very obvious, easily seen locations, but a variation on this practice is to purposely obscure the location of the senjafuda in order to protect it from exposure to wind and rain and thus prolong its presence.
== History == Senjafuda were first produced in the Heian period 794 to 1185 (平安時代 Heian jidai?) when shrine worshipers made pilgrimages to visits to many shrines and worship the Buddhist goddess of mercy, Kannon. They were not originally made of paper, they were first made from wooden slats that were hung from the gates of Kannon Temples by nails made of bamboo. The slats were carved out with the visitors’ name, area of origin and often included a prayer for a good life and afterlife. There are two styles of Senjafuda, the older style, daimei nosatsu and kokan nosatsu the new style. Daimei nosatsu are basic black ink on white paper. The ink used is called sumi and it is so strong that after the printed senjafuda was placed on the shrine or temple gate, years later when the paper peeled away, the ink remained. Which is why many shrine Kannushi (神主?) or shinshoku (神職?) do not like the use of senjafuda? Add to the fact that younger senjafuda practitioners now days, do not follow tradition and pray nor buy a stamp from the shrine before applying their senjafuda.
The later style senjafuda are called kokan nosatsu and they originated around Japans Edo period, 1603 to 1868 (江戸時代 Edo jidai?). They came about because during the beginning of the Edo period, the pilgrimages to shrines gained popularity and started what is known as senjamode which literally means a thousand shrine visits for good luck. Kokan nosatsu senjafuda are a lot more colorful and have rich patterns and designs. They were used as novelty items and more like trading cards or the business cards of today. Like most all things during the Edo period, they were regulated. Depending on your class and order in society, they could not contain too many colors if you were not upper class. Collectors who enjoyed the many designs and colors of senjafuda would meet to exchange with one another. First the meetings took place at private homes and then later were arranged for public places like restaurants and expensive tea houses. According to Vanishing Japan, by Elizabeth Kiritani, “the oldest surviving invitation card to one of these meetings dates back to 1799.” Of course the government found out about the meetings and enforced law upon them. This did not stop the meetings from taking place, and they continue to this day, collectors and aficionados alike meet to share and trade their own designs as well as admire others.
== Construction ==
Senjafuda used to be made from rice paper with ink called sumi. As for the paste, they used a rice mush. The pilgrims used to carry walking staffs for their long journeys, which doubled as an applicator for senjafuda. “The walking staff which doubles as a collapsible rod some of which are said to be up to 8 meters in length provides the means to paste senjafuda in hard to reach places. According to Vanishing Japan, by Elizabeth Kiritani,” They applied the paste with something called meotobake, two brushes about 30 degrees apart with a clip on the other side of the brushes. This is how they were able to paste the senjafuda in out of reach areas, leaving others to wonder exactly how they got up there.
Now senjafuda are made from printed paper and are rarely made traditional by wood block prints or ukiyo-e. They do make wooden slat senjafuda which are worn as a necklace or used for key chain and cell phone ordainments. The ones made from paper are pre-printed with common names and there are also machines that you can make custom senjafuda with adhesive backings.
[http://farm4.static.flickr.com/3355/3658609703_6cc7b7d1bd_b.jpg]
== Famous Figures == Some famous producers of senjafuda are Hiroshige, Eisen, Kunisada and Kuniyoshi. They mainly produce senjafuda for collectors because the process of ukiyoe printing is very expensive. Senrei Sekioka is Japans expert in Senjafuda design and trade.
== In recent years ==
''Senjafuda'' are also sold as stickers which do not require separate paste. As stickers they are also placed in books and on personal items for identification and decoration. A common criticism of the sticker version of senjafuda is that they are more difficult to peel off than their original pasted ancestors and thus can disfigure the underlying buildings when removed.
{{Japan-culture-stub}}
{{Uncategorized|date=June 2009}}
[[ja:千社札]]

The ones made from paper are pre-printed with common names and there are also machines that you can make custom senjafuda with adhesive backings.
[http://farm4.static.flickr.com/3355/3658609703_6cc7b7d1bd_b.jpg]
== Famous Figures == Some famous producers of senjafuda are Hiroshige, Eisen, Kunisada and Kuniyoshi. They mainly produce senjafuda for collectors because the process of ukiyoe printing is very expensive. Senrei Sekioka is Japans expert in Senjafuda design and trade.
== In recent years ==
''Senjafuda'' are also sold as stickers which do not require separate paste. As stickers they are also placed in books and on personal items for identification and decoration. A common criticism of the sticker version of senjafuda is that they are more difficult to peel off than their original pasted ancestors and thus can disfigure the underlying buildings when removed.
[[Category:Japanese culture]]
[[ja:千社札]]

{{orphan|date=March 2010}}
{{Unreferenced|date=November 2008}}
[[File:Gifu-kegonji0059.JPG|right|thumb|300px|''Senjafuda'' pasted on a shrine gate in [[Gifu]].]]
'''Senjafuda''' (千社札, literally "thousand shrine tags") are stickers or scraps of paper posted on the gates of [[Shinto shrine|shrines]] and [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] in [[Japan]]. The stickers bear the name of the worshipper, and can be purchased pre-printed with common names at temples and shrines throughout Japan, as well as at stationery stores and video game centres. ''Senjafuda'' were originally made from wooden slats, but have been made of paper since the [[Edo period]].
== Summary ==
A single senjafuda measures 1.6[[Cun (unit)|Sun]] (58mm) in width and 4.8 Sun (173mm) in height. This gives the senjafuda a ratio of 1:3. A frame is drawn inside this space which contains the lettering or pictures. In 1887 a measurement for this frame was also established as 48mm wide and 144mm tall.
Ordinarily, the designs were used to commemorate a visit to a temple or shrine and printed with simple monochromatic schemes, but eventually aesthetic sense gave way to colorful variations and designs. In the pleasure quarters of [[Kyoto]], colorful designs were employed on senjafuda and used in place of traditional business cards. This variation is called "hana-meishi" which roughly translated to "flower business card." Today, the "business card" use of senjafuda is the most common.
Senjafuda were primarily printed with [[Edomoji]], or Edo-period lettering styles, and pressed with the same traditional wooden boards used to produce [[ukiyo-e]] prints.
Stickers on shrines are often pasted in very obvious, easily seen locations, but a variation on this practice is to purposely obscure the location of the senjafuda in order to protect it from exposure to wind and rain and thus prolong its presence.
== History == Senjafuda were first produced in the Heian period 794 to 1185 (平安時代 Heian jidai?) when shrine worshipers made pilgrimages to visits to many shrines and worship the Buddhist goddess of mercy, Kannon. They were not originally made of paper, they were first made from wooden slats that were hung from the gates of Kannon Temples by nails made of bamboo. The slats were carved out with the visitors’ name, area of origin and often included a prayer for a good life and afterlife. There are two styles of Senjafuda, the older style, daimei nosatsu and kokan nosatsu the new style. Daimei nosatsu are basic black ink on white paper. The ink used is called sumi and it is so strong that after the printed senjafuda was placed on the shrine or temple gate, years later when the paper peeled away, the ink remained. Which is why many shrine Kannushi (神主?) or shinshoku (神職?) do not like the use of senjafuda? Add to the fact that younger senjafuda practitioners now days, do not follow tradition and pray nor buy a stamp from the shrine before applying their senjafuda.
The later style senjafuda are called kokan nosatsu and they originated around Japans Edo period, 1603 to 1868 (江戸時代 Edo jidai?). They came about because during the beginning of the Edo period, the pilgrimages to shrines gained popularity and started what is known as senjamode which literally means a thousand shrine visits for good luck. Kokan nosatsu senjafuda are a lot more colorful and have rich patterns and designs. They were used as novelty items and more like trading cards or the business cards of today. Like most all things during the Edo period, they were regulated. Depending on your class and order in society, they could not contain too many colors if you were not upper class. Collectors who enjoyed the many designs and colors of senjafuda would meet to exchange with one another. First the meetings took place at private homes and then later were arranged for public places like restaurants and expensive tea houses. According to Vanishing Japan, by Elizabeth Kiritani [http://books.google.co.jp/books?id=5_GGbiw1-y4C&pg=PA1&lpg=PA1&dq=vanishing+japan&source=bl&ots=vqjFnOgLhM&sig=QxOIQh0fX-spvbI0NRy4zt2f2yo&hl=ja&ei=VRqKSqb7F9KfkQW9rNgt&sa=X&oi=book_result&ct=result&resnum=1#v=onepage&q=&f=false], “the oldest surviving invitation card to one of these meetings dates back to 1799.” Of course the government found out about the meetings and enforced law upon them. This did not stop the meetings from taking place, and they continue to this day, collectors and aficionados alike meet to share and trade their own designs as well as admire others.
== Construction ==
Senjafuda used to be made from rice paper with ink called sumi. As for the paste, they used a rice mush. The pilgrims used to carry walking staffs for their long journeys, which doubled as an applicator for senjafuda. “The walking staff which doubles as a collapsible rod some of which are said to be up to 8 meters in length provides the means to paste senjafuda in hard to reach places. According to Vanishing Japan, by Elizabeth Kiritani,” They applied the paste with something called meotobake, two brushes about 30 degrees apart with a clip on the other side of the brushes. This is how they were able to paste the senjafuda in out of reach areas, leaving others to wonder exactly how they got up there.
Now senjafuda are made from printed paper and are rarely made traditional by wood block prints or ukiyo-e. They do make wooden slat senjafuda which are worn as a necklace or used for key chain and cell phone ordainments. The ones made from paper are pre-printed with common names and there are also machines that you can make custom senjafuda with adhesive backings.
[http://farm4.static.flickr.com/3355/3658609703_6cc7b7d1bd_b.jpg]
== Famous Figures == Some famous producers of senjafuda are Hiroshige, Eisen, Kunisada and Kuniyoshi. They mainly produce senjafuda for collectors because the process of ukiyoe printing is very expensive. Senrei Sekioka is Japans expert in Senjafuda design and trade.
== In recent years ==
''Senjafuda'' are also sold as stickers which do not require separate paste. As stickers they are also placed in books and on personal items for identification and decoration. A common criticism of the sticker version of senjafuda is that they are more difficult to peel off than their original pasted ancestors and thus can disfigure the underlying buildings when removed.
[[Category:Japanese culture]]
[[ja:千社札]]

Kokan nosatsu senjafuda are a lot more colorful and have rich patterns and designs. They were used as novelty items and more like trading cards or the business cards of today. Like most all things during the Edo period, they were regulated. Depending on your class and order in society, they could not contain too many colors if you were not upper class. Collectors who enjoyed the many designs and colors of senjafuda would meet to exchange with one another. First the meetings took place at private homes and then later were arranged for public places like restaurants and expensive tea houses. According to Vanishing Japan, by Elizabeth Kiritani [http://books.google.co.jp/books?id=5_GGbiw1-y4C&pg=PA1&lpg=PA1&dq=vanishing+japan&source=bl&ots=vqjFnOgLhM&sig=QxOIQh0fX-spvbI0NRy4zt2f2yo&hl=ja&ei=VRqKSqb7F9KfkQW9rNgt&sa=X&oi=book_result&ct=result&resnum=1#v=onepage&q=&f=false], “the oldest surviving invitation card to one of these meetings dates back to 1799.” Of course the government found out about the meetings and enforced law upon them. This did not stop the meetings from taking place, and they continue to this day, collectors and aficionados alike meet to share and trade their own designs as well as admire others.
== Construction ==
Senjafuda used to be made from rice paper with ink called sumi. As for the paste, they used a rice mush. The pilgrims used to carry walking staffs for their long journeys, which doubled as an applicator for senjafuda. “The walking staff which doubles as a collapsible rod some of which are said to be up to 8 meters in length provides the means to paste senjafuda in hard to reach places. According to Vanishing Japan, by Elizabeth Kiritani,” They applied the paste with something called meotobake, two brushes about 30 degrees apart with a clip on the other side of the brushes. This is how they were able to paste the senjafuda in out of reach areas, leaving others to wonder exactly how they got up there.
Now senjafuda are made from printed paper and are rarely made traditional by wood block prints or ukiyo-e. They do make wooden slat senjafuda which are worn as a necklace or used for key chain and cell phone ordainments. The ones made from paper are pre-printed with common names and there are also machines that you can make custom senjafuda with adhesive backings.
[http://farm4.static.flickr.com/3355/3658609703_6cc7b7d1bd_b.jpg]
== Famous Figures == Some famous producers of senjafuda are Hiroshige, Eisen, Kunisada and Kuniyoshi. They mainly produce senjafuda for collectors because the process of ukiyoe printing is very expensive. Senrei Sekioka is Japans expert in Senjafuda design and trade.
== In recent years ==
''Senjafuda'' are also sold as stickers which do not require separate paste. As stickers they are also placed in books and on personal items for identification and decoration. A common criticism of the sticker version of senjafuda is that they are more difficult to peel off than their original pasted ancestors and thus can disfigure the underlying buildings when removed.
==Reference== *Gordenker, Alice, "[http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/ek20101118wh.html So, What the Heck is That? Shrine tags]", ''[[Japan Times]]'', 18 November 2010, p. 13.</ref>
[[Category:Japanese culture]]
[[ja:千社札]] [[pl:Senja-fuda]]

{{orphan|date=March 2010}}
{{Unreferenced|date=November 2008}}
[[File:Gifu-kegonji0059.JPG|right|thumb|300px|''Senjafuda'' pasted on a shrine gate in [[Gifu, Gifu|Gifu]].]]
'''Senjafuda''' (千社札, literally "thousand shrine tags") are stickers or scraps of paper posted on the gates of [[Shinto shrine|shrines]] and [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] in [[Japan]]. The stickers bear the name of the worshipper, and can be purchased pre-printed with common names at temples and shrines throughout Japan, as well as at stationery stores and video game centres. ''Senjafuda'' were originally made from wooden slats, but have been made of paper since the [[Edo period]].
== Summary ==
A single senjafuda measures 1.6[[Cun (unit)|Sun]] (58mm) in width and 4.8 Sun (173mm) in height. This gives the senjafuda a ratio of 1:3. A frame is drawn inside this space which contains the lettering or pictures. In 1887 a measurement for this frame was also established as 48mm wide and 144mm tall.
Ordinarily, the designs were used to commemorate a visit to a temple or shrine and printed with simple monochromatic schemes, but eventually aesthetic sense gave way to colorful variations and designs. In the pleasure quarters of [[Kyoto]], colorful designs were employed on senjafuda and used in place of traditional business cards. This variation is called "hana-meishi" which roughly translated to "flower business card." Today, the "business card" use of senjafuda is the most common.
Senjafuda were primarily printed with [[Edomoji]], or Edo-period lettering styles, and pressed with the same traditional wooden boards used to produce [[ukiyo-e]] prints.
Stickers on shrines are often pasted in very obvious, easily seen locations, but a variation on this practice is to purposely obscure the location of the senjafuda in order to protect it from exposure to wind and rain and thus prolong its presence.
== History == Senjafuda were first produced in the Heian period 794 to 1185 (平安時代 Heian jidai?) when shrine worshipers made pilgrimages to visits to many shrines and worship the Buddhist goddess of mercy, Kannon. They were not originally made of paper, they were first made from wooden slats that were hung from the gates of Kannon Temples by nails made of bamboo. The slats were carved out with the visitors’ name, area of origin and often included a prayer for a good life and afterlife. There are two styles of Senjafuda, the older style, daimei nosatsu and kokan nosatsu the new style. Daimei nosatsu are basic black ink on white paper. The ink used is called sumi and it is so strong that after the printed senjafuda was placed on the shrine or temple gate, years later when the paper peeled away, the ink remained. Which is why many shrine Kannushi (神主?) or shinshoku (神職?) do not like the use of senjafuda? Add to the fact that younger senjafuda practitioners now days, do not follow tradition and pray nor buy a stamp from the shrine before applying their senjafuda.
The later style senjafuda are called kokan nosatsu and they originated around Japans Edo period, 1603 to 1868 (江戸時代 Edo jidai?). They came about because during the beginning of the Edo period, the pilgrimages to shrines gained popularity and started what is known as senjamode which literally means a thousand shrine visits for good luck. Kokan nosatsu senjafuda are a lot more colorful and have rich patterns and designs. They were used as novelty items and more like trading cards or the business cards of today. Like most all things during the Edo period, they were regulated. Depending on your class and order in society, they could not contain too many colors if you were not upper class. Collectors who enjoyed the many designs and colors of senjafuda would meet to exchange with one another. First the meetings took place at private homes and then later were arranged for public places like restaurants and expensive tea houses. This did not stop the meetings from taking place, and they continue to this day, collectors and aficionados alike meet to share and trade their own designs as well as admire others.
== Construction ==
Senjafuda used to be made from rice paper with ink called sumi. As for the paste, they used a rice mush. The pilgrims used to carry walking staffs for their long journeys, which doubled as an applicator for senjafuda. “The walking staff which doubles as a collapsible rod some of which are said to be up to 8 meters in length provides the means to paste senjafuda in hard to reach places. This is how they were able to paste the senjafuda in out of reach areas, leaving others to wonder exactly how they got up there.
Now senjafuda are made from printed paper and are rarely made traditional by wood block prints or ukiyo-e. They do make wooden slat senjafuda which are worn as a necklace or used for key chain and cell phone ordainments. The ones made from paper are pre-printed with common names and there are also machines that you can make custom senjafuda with adhesive backings.
[http://farm4.static.flickr.com/3355/3658609703_6cc7b7d1bd_b.jpg]
== Famous Figures == Some famous producers of senjafuda are Hiroshige, Eisen, Kunisada and Kuniyoshi. They mainly produce senjafuda for collectors because the process of ukiyoe printing is very expensive. Senrei Sekioka is Japans expert in Senjafuda design and trade.
== In recent years ==
''Senjafuda'' are also sold as stickers which do not require separate paste. As stickers they are also placed in books and on personal items for identification and decoration. A common criticism of the sticker version of senjafuda is that they are more difficult to peel off than their original pasted ancestors and thus can disfigure the underlying buildings when removed.
==Reference== *Gordenker, Alice, "[http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/ek20101118wh.html So, What the Heck is That? Shrine tags]", ''[[Japan Times]]'', 18 November 2010, p. 13.</ref>

[[fr:Senjafuda]] [[ja:千社札]] [[pl:Senjafuda]]

{{Unreferenced|date=November 2008}}
[[File:Gifu-kegonji0059.JPG|right|thumb|300px|''Senjafuda'' pasted on a shrine gate in [[Gifu, Gifu|Gifu]].]]
'''Senjafuda''' (千社札, literally "thousand shrine tags") are stickers or scraps of paper posted on the gates of [[Shinto shrine|shrines]] and [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] in [[Japan]]. The stickers bear the name of the worshipper, and can be purchased pre-printed with common names at temples and shrines throughout Japan, as well as at stationery stores and video game centres. ''Senjafuda'' were originally made from wooden slats, but have been made of paper since the [[Edo period]].
== Summary ==
A single senjafuda measures 1.6[[Cun (unit)|Sun]] (58mm) in width and 4.8 Sun (173mm) in height. This gives the senjafuda a ratio of 1:3. A frame is drawn inside this space which contains the lettering or pictures. In 1887 a measurement for this frame was also established as 48mm wide and 144mm tall.
Ordinarily, the designs were used to commemorate a visit to a temple or shrine and printed with simple monochromatic schemes, but eventually aesthetic sense gave way to colorful variations and designs. In the pleasure quarters of [[Kyoto (city)|Kyoto]], colorful designs were employed on senjafuda and used in place of traditional business cards. This variation is called "hana-meishi" which roughly translated to "flower business card." Today, the "business card" use of senjafuda is the most common.
Senjafuda were primarily printed with [[Edomoji]], or Edo-period lettering styles, and pressed with the same traditional wooden boards used to produce [[ukiyo-e]] prints.
Stickers on shrines are often pasted in very obvious, easily seen locations, but a variation on this practice is to purposely obscure the location of the senjafuda in order to protect it from exposure to wind and rain and thus prolong its presence.
== History == Senjafuda were first produced in the Heian period 794 to 1185 (平安時代 Heian jidai?) when shrine worshipers made pilgrimages to visits to many shrines and worship the Buddhist goddess of mercy, Kannon. They were not originally made of paper, they were first made from wooden slats that were hung from the gates of Kannon Temples by nails made of bamboo. The slats were carved out with the visitors’ name, area of origin and often included a prayer for a good life and afterlife. There are two styles of Senjafuda, the older style, daimei nosatsu and kokan nosatsu the new style. Daimei nosatsu are basic black ink on white paper. The ink used is called sumi and it is so strong that after the printed senjafuda was placed on the shrine or temple gate, years later when the paper peeled away, the ink remained. Which is why many shrine Kannushi (神主?) or shinshoku (神職?) do not like the use of senjafuda? Add to the fact that younger senjafuda practitioners now days, do not follow tradition and pray nor buy a stamp from the shrine before applying their senjafuda.
The later style senjafuda are called kokan nosatsu and they originated around Japans Edo period, 1603 to 1868 (江戸時代 Edo jidai?). They came about because during the beginning of the Edo period, the pilgrimages to shrines gained popularity and started what is known as senjamode which literally means a thousand shrine visits for good luck. Kokan nosatsu senjafuda are a lot more colorful and have rich patterns and designs. They were used as novelty items and more like trading cards or the business cards of today. Like most all things during the Edo period, they were regulated. Depending on your class and order in society, they could not contain too many colors if you were not upper class. Collectors who enjoyed the many designs and colors of senjafuda would meet to exchange with one another. First the meetings took place at private homes and then later were arranged for public places like restaurants and expensive tea houses. According to Vanishing Japan, by Elizabeth Kiritani [http://books.google.co.jp/books?id=5_GGbiw1-y4C&pg=PA1&lpg=PA1&dq=vanishing+japan&source=bl&ots=vqjFnOgLhM&sig=QxOIQh0fX-spvbI0NRy4zt2f2yo&hl=ja&ei=VRqKSqb7F9KfkQW9rNgt&sa=X&oi=book_result&ct=result&resnum=1#v=onepage&q=&f=false], “the oldest surviving invitation card to one of these meetings dates back to 1799.” Of course the government found out about the meetings and enforced law upon them. This did not stop the meetings from taking place, and they continue to this day, collectors and aficionados alike meet to share and trade their own designs as well as admire others.
== Construction ==
Senjafuda used to be made from rice paper with ink called sumi. As for the paste, they used a rice mush. The pilgrims used to carry walking staffs for their long journeys, which doubled as an applicator for senjafuda. “The walking staff which doubles as a collapsible rod some of which are said to be up to 8 meters in length provides the means to paste senjafuda in hard to reach places. According to Vanishing Japan, by Elizabeth Kiritani,” They applied the paste with something called meotobake, two brushes about 30 degrees apart with a clip on the other side of the brushes. This is how they were able to paste the senjafuda in out of reach areas, leaving others to wonder exactly how they got up there.
Now senjafuda are made from printed paper and are rarely made traditional by wood block prints or ukiyo-e. They do make wooden slat senjafuda which are worn as a necklace or used for key chain and cell phone ordainments. The ones made from paper are pre-printed with common names and there are also machines that you can make custom senjafuda with adhesive backings.
[http://farm4.static.flickr.com/3355/3658609703_6cc7b7d1bd_b.jpg]
== Famous Figures == Some famous producers of senjafuda are Hiroshige, Eisen, Kunisada and Kuniyoshi. They mainly produce senjafuda for collectors because the process of ukiyoe printing is very expensive. Senrei Sekioka is Japans expert in Senjafuda design and trade.
== In recent years ==
''Senjafuda'' are also sold as stickers which do not require separate paste. As stickers they are also placed in books and on personal items for identification and decoration. A common criticism of the sticker version of senjafuda is that they are more difficult to peel off than their original pasted ancestors and thus can disfigure the underlying buildings when removed.
==References== *Gordenker, Alice, "[http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/ek20101118wh.html So, What the Heck is That? Shrine tags]", ''[[Japan Times]]'', 18 November 2010, p. 13.</ref>
[[Category:Japanese culture]]
[[fr:Senjafuda]] [[ja:千社札]] [[pl:Senjafuda]]

{{Unreferenced|date=November 2008}}

The later style senjafuda are called kokan nosatsu and they originated around Japans Edo period, 1603 to 1868 (江戸時代 Edo jidai?). They came about because during the beginning of the Edo period, the pilgrimages to shrines gained popularity and started what is known as senjamode which literally means a thousand shrine visits for good luck. Kokan nosatsu senjafuda are a lot more colorful and have rich patterns and designs. They were used as novelty items and more like trading cards or the business cards of today. Like most all things during the Edo period, they were regulated. Depending on your class and order in society, they could not contain too many colors if you were not upper class. Collectors who enjoyed the many designs and colors of senjafuda would meet to exchange with one another. First the meetings took place at private homes and then later were arranged for public places like restaurants and expensive tea houses. According to Vanishing Japan, by Elizabeth Kiritani [http://books.google.co.jp/books?id=5_GGbiw1-y4C&pg=PA1&lpg=PA1&dq=vanishing+japan&source=bl&ots=vqjFnOgLhM&sig=QxOIQh0fX-spvbI0NRy4zt2f2yo&hl=ja&ei=VRqKSqb7F9KfkQW9rNgt&sa=X&oi=book_result&ct=result&resnum=1#v=onepage&q=&f=false], “the oldest surviving invitation card to one of these meetings dates back to 1799.” Of course the government found out about the meetings and enforced law upon them. This did not stop the meetings from taking place, and they continue to this day, collectors and aficionados alike meet to share and trade their own designs as well as admire others.
== Construction ==
Senjafuda used to be made from rice paper with ink called sumi. As for the paste, they used a rice mush. The pilgrims used to carry walking staffs for their long journeys, which doubled as an applicator for senjafuda. “The walking staff which doubles as a collapsible rod some of which are said to be up to 8 meters in length provides the means to paste senjafuda in hard to reach places. According to Vanishing Japan, by Elizabeth Kiritani,” They applied the paste with something called meotobake, two brushes about 30 degrees apart with a clip on the other side of the brushes. This is how they were able to paste the senjafuda in out of reach areas, leaving others to wonder exactly how they got up there.
Now senjafuda are made from printed paper and are rarely made traditional by wood block prints or ukiyo-e. They do make wooden slat senjafuda which are worn as a necklace or used for key chain and cell phone ordainments. The ones made from paper are pre-printed with common names and there are also machines that you can make custom senjafuda with adhesive backings.
[http://farm4.static.flickr.com/3355/3658609703_6cc7b7d1bd_b.jpg]
== Famous Figures == Some famous producers of senjafuda are Hiroshige, Eisen, Kunisada and Kuniyoshi. They mainly produce senjafuda for collectors because the process of ukiyoe printing is very expensive. Senrei Sekioka is Japans expert in Senjafuda design and trade.
== In recent years ==
''Senjafuda'' are also sold as stickers which do not require separate paste. As stickers they are also placed in books and on personal items for identification and decoration. A common criticism of the sticker version of senjafuda is that they are more difficult to peel off than their original pasted ancestors and thus can disfigure the underlying buildings when removed.
==References== *Gordenker, Alice, "[http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/ek20101118wh.html So, What the Heck is That? Shrine tags]", ''[[Japan Times]]'', 18 November 2010, p. 13.
[[Category:Japanese culture]]

{{Unreferenced|date=November 2008}}
[[File:Gifu-kegonji0059.JPG|right|thumb|300px|''Senjafuda'' pasted on a shrine gate in [[Gifu, Gifu|Gifu]].]]
'''Senjafuda''' (千社札, literally "thousand shrine tags") are stickers or scraps of paper posted on the gates of [[Shinto shrine|shrines]] and [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] in [[Japan]]. The stickers bear the name of the worshipper, and can be purchased pre-printed with common names at temples and shrines throughout Japan, as well as at stationery stores and video game centres. ''Senjafuda'' were originally made from wooden slats, but have been made of paper since the [[Edo period]].
== Summary ==
A single senjafuda measures 1.6[[Cun (unit)|Sun]] (58mm) in width and 4.8 Sun (173mm) in height. This gives the senjafuda a ratio of 1:3. A frame is drawn inside this space which contains the lettering or pictures. In 1887 a measurement for this frame was also established as 48mm wide and 144mm tall.
Ordinarily, the designs were used to commemorate a visit to a temple or shrine and printed with simple monochromatic schemes, but eventually aesthetic sense gave way to colorful variations and designs. In the pleasure quarters of [[Kyoto]], colorful designs were employed on senjafuda and used in place of traditional business cards. This variation is called "hana-meishi" which roughly translated to "flower business card." Today, the "business card" use of senjafuda is the most common.
Senjafuda were primarily printed with [[Edomoji]], or Edo-period lettering styles, and pressed with the same traditional wooden boards used to produce [[ukiyo-e]] prints.
Stickers on shrines are often pasted in very obvious, easily seen locations, but a variation on this practice is to purposely obscure the location of the senjafuda in order to protect it from exposure to wind and rain and thus prolong its presence.
== History == Senjafuda were first produced in the Heian period 794 to 1185 (平安時代 Heian jidai?) when shrine worshipers made pilgrimages to visits to many shrines and worship the Buddhist goddess of mercy, Kannon. They were not originally made of paper, they were first made from wooden slats that were hung from the gates of Kannon Temples by nails made of bamboo. The slats were carved out with the visitors’ name, area of origin and often included a prayer for a good life and afterlife. There are two styles of Senjafuda, the older style, daimei nosatsu and kokan nosatsu the new style. Daimei nosatsu are basic black ink on white paper. The ink used is called sumi and it is so strong that after the printed senjafuda was placed on the shrine or temple gate, years later when the paper peeled away, the ink remained. Which is why many shrine Kannushi (神主?) or shinshoku (神職?) do not like the use of senjafuda? Add to the fact that younger senjafuda practitioners nowadays, do not follow tradition and pray nor buy a stamp from the shrine before applying their senjafuda.
The later style senjafuda are called kokan nosatsu and they originated around Japan's Edo period, 1603 to 1868 (江戸時代 Edo jidai?). They came about because during the beginning of the Edo period, the pilgrimages to shrines gained popularity and started what is known as senjamode which literally means a thousand shrine visits for good luck. Kokan nosatsu senjafuda are a lot more colorful and have rich patterns and designs. They were used as novelty items and more like trading cards or the business cards of today. Like most all things during the Edo period, they were regulated. Depending on your class and order in society, they could not contain too many colors if you were not upper class. Collectors who enjoyed the many designs and colors of senjafuda would meet to exchange with one another. First the meetings took place at private homes and then later were arranged for public places like restaurants and expensive tea houses. According to Vanishing Japan, by Elizabeth Kiritani [http://books.google.co.jp/books?id=5_GGbiw1-y4C&pg=PA1&lpg=PA1&dq=vanishing+japan&source=bl&ots=vqjFnOgLhM&sig=QxOIQh0fX-spvbI0NRy4zt2f2yo&hl=ja&ei=VRqKSqb7F9KfkQW9rNgt&sa=X&oi=book_result&ct=result&resnum=1#v=onepage&q=&f=false], “the oldest surviving invitation card to one of these meetings dates back to 1799.” Of course the government found out about the meetings and enforced law upon them. This did not stop the meetings from taking place, and they continue to this day, collectors and aficionados alike meet to share and trade their own designs as well as admire others.
== Construction ==
Senjafuda used to be made from rice paper with ink called sumi. As for the paste, they used a rice mush. The pilgrims used to carry walking staffs for their long journeys, which doubled as an applicator for senjafuda. “The walking staff which doubles as a collapsible rod some of which are said to be up to 8 meters in length provides the means to paste senjafuda in hard to reach places. According to Vanishing Japan, by Elizabeth Kiritani,” They applied the paste with something called meotobake, two brushes about 30 degrees apart with a clip on the other side of the brushes. This is how they were able to paste the senjafuda in out of reach areas, leaving others to wonder exactly how they got up there.
Now senjafuda are made from printed paper and are rarely made traditional by wood block prints or ukiyo-e. They do make wooden slat senjafuda which are worn as a necklace or used for key chain and cell phone ordainments. The ones made from paper are pre-printed with common names and there are also machines that you can make custom senjafuda with adhesive backings.
[http://farm4.static.flickr.com/3355/3658609703_6cc7b7d1bd_b.jpg]
== Famous Figures == Some famous producers of senjafuda are Hiroshige, Eisen, Kunisada and Kuniyoshi. They mainly produce senjafuda for collectors because the process of ukiyoe printing is very expensive. Senrei Sekioka is Japan's expert in Senjafuda design and trade.
== In recent years ==
''Senjafuda'' are also sold as stickers which do not require separate paste. As stickers they are also placed in books and on personal items for identification and decoration. A common criticism of the sticker version of senjafuda is that they are more difficult to peel off than their original pasted ancestors and thus can disfigure the underlying buildings when removed.
==References== *Gordenker, Alice, "[http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/ek20101118wh.html So, What the Heck is That? Shrine tags]", ''[[Japan Times]]'', 18 November 2010, p. 13.
[[Category:Japanese culture]]

== Summary ==
A single senjafuda measures 1.6 [[Cun (unit)|Sun]] (58mm) in width and 4.8 Sun (173mm) in height. This gives the senjafuda a ratio of 1:3. A frame is drawn inside this space which contains the lettering or pictures. In 1887 a measurement for this frame was also established as 48mm wide and 144mm tall.
Ordinarily, the designs were used to commemorate a visit to a temple or shrine and printed with simple monochromatic schemes, but eventually aesthetic sense gave way to colorful variations and designs. In the pleasure quarters of [[Kyoto]], colorful designs were employed on senjafuda and used in place of traditional business cards. This variation is called "hana-meishi" which roughly translated to "flower business card." Today, the "business card" use of senjafuda is the most common.
Senjafuda were primarily printed with [[Edomoji]], or Edo-period lettering styles, and pressed with the same traditional wooden boards used to produce [[ukiyo-e]] prints.
Stickers on shrines are often pasted in very obvious, easily seen locations, but a variation on this practice is to purposely obscure the location of the senjafuda in order to protect it from exposure to wind and rain and thus prolong its presence.
== History == Senjafuda were first produced in the [[Heian period]] 794 to 1185when shrine worshipers made pilgrimages to visits to many shrines and worship the Buddhist goddess of mercy, [[Kannon]]. They were not originally made of paper, they were first made from wooden slats that were hung from the gates of Kannon Temples by nails made of bamboo. The slats were carved out with the visitors’ name, area of origin and often included a prayer for a good life and afterlife. There are two styles of Senjafuda, the older style, daimei nosatsu and kokan nosatsu the new style. Daimei nosatsu are basic black ink on white paper. The ink used is called sumi and it is so strong that after the printed senjafuda was placed on the shrine or temple gate, years later when the paper peeled away, the ink remained. Which is why many shrine [[Kannushi]] or [[shinshoku]] do not like the use of senjafuda. Add to the fact that younger senjafuda practitioners nowadays, do not follow tradition and pray nor buy a stamp from the shrine before applying their senjafuda.
The later style senjafuda are called kokan nosatsu and they originated around Japan's [[Edo period]], 1603 to 1868. They came about because during the beginning of the Edo period, the pilgrimages to shrines gained popularity and started what is known as senjamode which literally means a thousand shrine visits for good luck. Kokan nosatsu senjafuda are a lot more colorful and have rich patterns and designs. They were used as novelty items and more like trading cards or the business cards of today. Like most all things during the Edo period, they were regulated. Depending on your class and order in society, they could not contain too many colors if you were not upper class. Collectors who enjoyed the many designs and colors of senjafuda would meet to exchange with one another. First the meetings took place at private homes and then later were arranged for public places like restaurants and expensive tea houses. According to Vanishing Japan, by Elizabeth Kiritani [http://books.google.co.jp/books?id=5_GGbiw1-y4C&pg=PA1&lpg=PA1&dq=vanishing+japan&source=bl&ots=vqjFnOgLhM&sig=QxOIQh0fX-spvbI0NRy4zt2f2yo&hl=ja&ei=VRqKSqb7F9KfkQW9rNgt&sa=X&oi=book_result&ct=result&resnum=1#v=onepage&q=&f=false], “the oldest surviving invitation card to one of these meetings dates back to 1799.” Of course the government found out about the meetings and enforced law upon them. This did not stop the meetings from taking place, and they continue to this day, collectors and aficionados alike meet to share and trade their own designs as well as admire others.
== Construction ==
Senjafuda used to be made from rice paper with ink called sumi. As for the paste, they used a rice mush. The pilgrims used to carry walking staffs for their long journeys, which doubled as an applicator for senjafuda. “The walking staff which doubles as a collapsible rod some of which are said to be up to 8 meters in length provides the means to paste senjafuda in hard to reach places. According to Vanishing Japan, by Elizabeth Kiritani,” They applied the paste with something called meotobake, two brushes about 30 degrees apart with a clip on the other side of the brushes. This is how they were able to paste the senjafuda in out of reach areas, leaving others to wonder exactly how they got up there.
Now senjafuda are made from printed paper and are rarely made traditional by wood block prints or ukiyo-e. They do make wooden slat senjafuda which are worn as a necklace or used for key chain and cell phone ordainments. The ones made from paper are pre-printed with common names and there are also machines that you can make custom senjafuda with adhesive backings.
== Famous figures == Some famous producers of senjafuda are Hiroshige, Eisen, Kunisada and Kuniyoshi. They mainly produce senjafuda for collectors because the process of ukiyoe printing is very expensive. Senrei Sekioka is Japan's expert in Senjafuda design and trade.
== In recent years ==
''Senjafuda'' are also sold as stickers which do not require separate paste. As stickers they are also placed in books and on personal items for identification and decoration. A common criticism of the sticker version of senjafuda is that they are more difficult to peel off than their original pasted ancestors and thus can disfigure the underlying buildings when removed.
==References== *Gordenker, Alice, "[http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/ek20101118wh.html So, What the Heck is That? Shrine tags]", ''[[Japan Times]]'', 18 November 2010, p. 13.
{{Authority control}} [[Category:Japanese culture]]

A frame is drawn inside this space which contains the lettering or pictures. In 1887 a measurement for this frame was also established as 48mm wide and 144mm tall.
Ordinarily, the designs were used to commemorate a visit to a temple or shrine and printed with simple monochromatic schemes, but eventually aesthetic sense gave way to colorful variations and designs. In the pleasure quarters of [[Kyoto]], colorful designs were employed on senjafuda and used in place of traditional business cards. This variation is called "hana-meishi" which roughly translated to "flower business card." Today, the "business card" use of senjafuda is the most common.

They were not originally made of paper, they were first made from wooden slats that were hung from the gates of Kannon Temples by nails made of bamboo. The slats were carved out with the visitors’ name, area of origin and often included a prayer for a good life and afterlife. There are two styles of Senjafuda, the older style, daimei nosatsu and kokan nosatsu the new style. Daimei nosatsu are basic black ink on white paper. The ink used is called sumi and it is so strong that after the printed senjafuda was placed on the shrine or temple gate, years later when the paper peeled away, the ink remained. Which is why many shrine [[Kannushi]] or [[shinshoku]] do not like the use of senjafuda. Add to the fact that younger senjafuda practitioners nowadays, do not follow tradition and pray nor buy a stamp from the shrine before applying their senjafuda.
The later style senjafuda are called kokan nosatsu and they originated around Japan's [[Edo period]], 1603 to 1868. They came about because during the beginning of the Edo period, the pilgrimages to shrines gained popularity and started what is known as senjamode which literally means a thousand shrine visits for good luck. Kokan nosatsu senjafuda are a lot more colorful and have rich patterns and designs. They were used as novelty items and more like trading cards or the business cards of today. Like most all things during the Edo period, they were regulated. Depending on your class and order in society, they could not contain too many colors if you were not upper class. First the meetings took place at private homes and then later were arranged for public places like restaurants and expensive tea houses. U.S. collector and Japanese anthropologist [[Frederick Starr]] was a turn-of-the-century collector and avid participant in senjafuda- or nōsatsu-kai (votive slip exchange clubs), so much so that he was given the name Dr. Ofuda. He collected tens of thousands of slips, and a fellow collector and popular art enthusiast, [[Gertrude Bass Warner]], purchased much of his collection. It currently resides at the University of Oregon Knight Library Special Collections & University Archives, part of the Gertrude Bass Warner Collection.<ref>{{cite web|last1=McDowell|first1=Kevin|title=Rare Collection: Nōsatsu Japanese Shrine and Temple Votive Slips|url=https://blogs.uoregon.edu/scua/2016/05/09/rare-collection-nosatsu-japanese-shrine-and-temple-votive-slips/|website=Upbound UO Blog|publisher=Special Collections & University Archives, University of Oregon|accessdate=3 May 2017}}</ref>
== Construction ==
Senjafuda used to be made from rice paper with ink called sumi. As for the paste, they used a rice mush. The pilgrims used to carry walking staffs for their long journeys, which doubled as an applicator for senjafuda. “The walking staff which doubles as a collapsible rod some of which are said to be up to 8 meters in length provides the means to paste senjafuda in hard to reach places. According to Vanishing Japan, by Elizabeth Kiritani,” They applied the paste with something called meotobake, two brushes about 30 degrees apart with a clip on the other side of the brushes. This is how they were able to paste the senjafuda in out of reach areas, leaving others to wonder exactly how they got up there.
Now senjafuda are made from printed paper and are rarely made traditional by wood block prints or ukiyo-e. They do make wooden slat senjafuda which are worn as a necklace or used for key chain and cell phone ordainments. The ones made from paper are pre-printed with common names and there are also machines that you can make custom senjafuda with adhesive backings.
== Famous figures == Some famous producers of senjafuda are Hiroshige, Eisen, Kunisada and Kuniyoshi. They mainly produce senjafuda for collectors because the process of ukiyoe printing is very expensive. Senrei Sekioka is Japan's expert in Senjafuda design and trade.
== In recent years ==
''Senjafuda'' are also sold as stickers which do not require separate paste. As stickers they are also placed in books and on personal items for identification and decoration. A common criticism of the sticker version of senjafuda is that they are more difficult to peel off than their original pasted ancestors and thus can disfigure the underlying buildings when removed.
==References== *Gordenker, Alice, "[http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/ek20101118wh.html So, What the Heck is That? Shrine tags]", ''[[Japan Times]]'', 18 November 2010, p. 13.

{{Unreferenced|date=November 2008}}
[[File:Gifu-kegonji0059.JPG|right|thumb|300px|''Senjafuda'' pasted on a shrine gate in [[Gifu, Gifu|Gifu]].]]
{{Nihongo|'''Senjafuda'''|千社札|4 = literally "thousand shrine tags"}} are stickers or scraps of paper posted on the gates of [[Shinto shrine|shrines]] and [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] in [[Japan]]. The stickers bear the name of the worshipper, and can be purchased pre-printed with common names at temples and shrines throughout Japan, as well as at stationery stores and video game centres. ''Senjafuda'' were originally made from wooden slats, but have been made of paper since the [[Edo period]].
== Summary ==
A single senjafuda measures 1.6 [[Cun (unit)|Sun]] (58mm) in width and 4.8 Sun (173mm) in height. This gives the senjafuda a ratio of 1:3. A frame is drawn inside this space which contains the lettering or pictures. In 1887 a measurement for this frame was also established as 48mm wide and 144mm tall.
Ordinarily, the designs were used to commemorate a visit to a temple or shrine and printed with simple monochromatic schemes, but eventually aesthetic sense gave way to colorful variations and designs. In the pleasure quarters of [[Kyoto]], colorful designs were employed on senjafuda and used in place of traditional business cards. This variation is called "hana-meishi" which roughly translated to "flower business card." Today, the "business card" use of senjafuda is the most common.
Senjafuda were primarily printed with [[Edomoji]], or Edo-period lettering styles, and pressed with the same traditional wooden boards used to produce [[ukiyo-e]] prints.
Stickers on shrines are often pasted in very obvious, easily seen locations, but a variation on this practice is to purposely obscure the location of the senjafuda in order to protect it from exposure to wind and rain and thus prolong its presence.
== History == Senjafuda were first produced in the [[Heian period]] 794 to 1185 when shrine worshipers made pilgrimages to visits to many shrines and worship the Buddhist goddess of mercy, [[Kannon]]. They were not originally made of paper, they were first made from wooden slats that were hung from the gates of Kannon Temples by nails made of bamboo. The slats were carved out with the visitors’ name, area of origin and often included a prayer for a good life and afterlife. There are two styles of Senjafuda, the older style, daimei nosatsu and kokan nosatsu the new style. Daimei nosatsu are basic black ink on white paper. The ink used is called sumi and it is so strong that after the printed senjafuda was placed on the shrine or temple gate, years later when the paper peeled away, the ink remained. Which is why many shrine [[Kannushi]] or [[shinshoku]] do not like the use of senjafuda. Add to the fact that younger senjafuda practitioners nowadays, do not follow tradition and pray nor buy a stamp from the shrine before applying their senjafuda.
The later style senjafuda are called kokan nosatsu and they originated around Japan's [[Edo period]], 1603 to 1868. They came about because during the beginning of the Edo period, the pilgrimages to shrines gained popularity and started what is known as senjamode which literally means a thousand shrine visits for good luck. Kokan nosatsu senjafuda are a lot more colorful and have rich patterns and designs. They were used as novelty items and more like trading cards or the business cards of today. Like most all things during the Edo period, they were regulated. Depending on your class and order in society, they could not contain too many colors if you were not upper class. Collectors who enjoyed the many designs and colors of senjafuda would meet to exchange with one another. First the meetings took place at private homes and then later were arranged for public places like restaurants and expensive tea houses. According to Vanishing Japan, by Elizabeth Kiritani [https://books.google.com/books?id=5_GGbiw1-y4C&pg=PA1&lpg=PA1&dq=vanishing+japan&source=bl&ots=vqjFnOgLhM&sig=QxOIQh0fX-spvbI0NRy4zt2f2yo&hl=ja&ei=VRqKSqb7F9KfkQW9rNgt&sa=X&oi=book_result&ct=result&resnum=1#v=onepage&q=&f=false], “the oldest surviving invitation card to one of these meetings dates back to 1799.” Of course the government found out about the meetings and enforced law upon them. This did not stop the meetings from taking place, and they continue to this day, collectors and aficionados alike meet to share and trade their own designs as well as admire others.
U.S. collector and Japanese anthropologist [[Frederick Starr]] was a turn-of-the-century collector and avid participant in senjafuda- or nōsatsu-kai (votive slip exchange clubs), so much so that he was given the name Dr. Ofuda. He collected tens of thousands of slips, and a fellow collector and popular art enthusiast, [[Gertrude Bass Warner]], purchased much of his collection. It currently resides at the University of Oregon Knight Library Special Collections & University Archives, part of the Gertrude Bass Warner Collection, and examples are viewable online at UO Oregon Digital.<ref>{{cite web|last1=McDowell|first1=Kevin|title=Gertrude Bass Warner Collection of Japanese Shrine and Temple Votive Slips (nōsatsu)|url=https://oregondigital.org/sets/gb-warner-nosatsu|website=Oregon Digital|publisher=University of Oregon|accessdate=3 May 2017}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|last1=McDowell|first1=Kevin|title=Rare Collection: Nōsatsu Japanese Shrine and Temple Votive Slips|url=https://blogs.uoregon.edu/scua/2016/05/09/rare-collection-nosatsu-japanese-shrine-and-temple-votive-slips/|website=Upbound UO Blog|publisher=Special Collections & University Archives, University of Oregon|accessdate=3 May 2017}}</ref>

According to Vanishing Japan, by Elizabeth Kiritani,” They applied the paste with something called meotobake, two brushes about 30 degrees apart with a clip on the other side of the brushes. This is how they were able to paste the senjafuda in out of reach areas, leaving others to wonder exactly how they got up there.
Now senjafuda are made from printed paper and are rarely made traditional by wood block prints or ukiyo-e. They do make wooden slat senjafuda which are worn as a necklace or used for key chain and cell phone ordainments. The ones made from paper are pre-printed with common names and there are also machines that you can make custom senjafuda with adhesive backings.
== Famous figures == Some famous producers of senjafuda are Hiroshige, Eisen, Kunisada and Kuniyoshi. They mainly produce senjafuda for collectors because the process of ukiyoe printing is very expensive. Senrei Sekioka is Japan's expert in Senjafuda design and trade.
== In recent years ==
''Senjafuda'' are also sold as stickers which do not require separate paste. As stickers they are also placed in books and on personal items for identification and decoration. A common criticism of the sticker version of senjafuda is that they are more difficult to peel off than their original pasted ancestors and thus can disfigure the underlying buildings when removed.
==References== *Gordenker, Alice, "[http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/ek20101118wh.html So, What the Heck is That? Shrine tags]", ''[[Japan Times]]'', 18 November 2010, p. 13.

It currently resides at the University of Oregon Knight Library Special Collections & University Archives, part of the Gertrude Bass Warner Collection, and examples are viewable online at UO Oregon Digital.<ref>{{cite web|last1=McDowell|first1=Kevin|title=Gertrude Bass Warner Collection of Japanese Shrine and Temple Votive Slips (nōsatsu)|url=https://oregondigital.org/sets/gb-warner-nosatsu|website=Oregon Digital|publisher=University of Oregon|accessdate=3 May 2017}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|last1=McDowell|first1=Kevin|title=Rare Collection: Nōsatsu Japanese Shrine and Temple Votive Slips|url=https://blogs.uoregon.edu/scua/2016/05/09/rare-collection-nosatsu-japanese-shrine-and-temple-votive-slips/|website=Upbound UO Blog|publisher=Special Collections & University Archives, University of Oregon|accessdate=3 May 2017}}</ref>
== Construction ==
Senjafuda used to be made from rice paper with ink called sumi. As for the paste, they used a rice mush. The pilgrims used to carry walking staffs for their long journeys, which doubled as an applicator for senjafuda. “The walking staff which doubles as a collapsible rod some of which are said to be up to 8 meters in length provides the means to paste senjafuda in hard to reach places. According to Vanishing Japan, by Elizabeth Kiritani,” They applied the paste with something called meotobake, two brushes about 30 degrees apart with a clip on the other side of the brushes. This is how they were able to paste the senjafuda in out of reach areas, leaving others to wonder exactly how they got up there.
Now senjafuda are made from printed paper and are rarely made traditional by wood block prints or ukiyo-e. They do make wooden slat senjafuda which are worn as a necklace or used for key chain and cell phone ordainments. The ones made from paper are pre-printed with common names and there are also machines that you can make custom senjafuda with adhesive backings.
== Famous figures == Some famous producers of senjafuda are Hiroshige, Eisen, Kunisada, Kuniyoshi. They mainly produced senjafuda for collectors because the process of ukiyoe printing is very expensive. Senrei Sekioka was one of the foremost Japanese experts of Senjafuda history. Iseman and Frederick Starr were important members of the nosatsu-kai during the Meiji and Taisho eras.
== In recent years ==
''Senjafuda'' are also sold as stickers which do not require separate paste. As stickers they are also placed in books and on personal items for identification and decoration. A common criticism of the sticker version of senjafuda is that they are more difficult to peel off than their original pasted ancestors and thus can disfigure the underlying buildings when removed.
