== External links == {{commonscat-inline}} *[https://glam.uoregon.edu/yokaisenjafuda/page/world-of-senjafuda World of Senjafuda] University of Oregon *[https://www.miniaturejapanesewoodblocks.net/gallery-6-senjafuda-votive-slips Gallery of senjafuda] Christenson Collection of Miniature Japanese Woodblock Prints
{{ukiyo-e}} {{Authority control}}
[[Category:Japanese culture]] [[Category:Buddhism in the Edo period]] [[Category:Ukiyo-e]] [[Category:Buddhist religious objects]] [[Category:Shinto cult objects]]

{{Refimprove|date=February 2018}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} [[File:八坂神社 (4472701259).jpg|thumb|Examples on a ceiling at [[Yasaka Shrine]]]] [[File:Gifu-kegonji0059.JPG|right|thumb|300px|{{transl|ja|Senjafuda}} pasted on a shrine gate in [[Gifu, Gifu|Gifu]].]]
{{Nihongo3|literally "thousand shrine tags"|千社札|'''Senjafuda'''}} are votive slips or placards posted on the gates or buildings of [[Shinto shrine]]s and [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] in [[Japan]]. The stickers bear the name of the worshipper, and can be purchased pre-printed with common names at temples and shrines throughout Japan, as well as at stationery stores and video game centres. {{transl|ja|Senjafuda}} were originally made from wooden slats, but have been made of paper since the [[Edo period]].
A single senjafuda measures 1.6 [[Cun (unit)|Sun]] ({{convert|58|mm|abbr=on}}) in width and 4.8 Sun ({{convert|173|mm|abbr=on}}) in height. This gives the {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} a ratio of 1:3. A frame is drawn inside this space which contains the lettering or pictures. In 1887, a measurement for this frame was also established as {{convert|48|mm|abbr=on}} wide and {{convert|144|mm|abbr=on}} tall.
Ordinarily, the designs were used to commemorate a visit to a temple or shrine and printed with simple monochromatic schemes, but eventually aesthetic sense gave way to colorful variations and designs. In the pleasure quarters of [[Kyoto]], colorful designs were employed on {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} and used in place of traditional business cards. This variation is called {{transl|ja|"hana-meishi"}} which roughly translated to "flower business card." Today, the "business card" use of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} is the most common.
{{transl|ja|Senjafuda}} were primarily printed with {{transl|ja|[[Edomoji]]}}, or Edo-period lettering styles, and pressed with the same traditional wooden boards used to produce {{transl|ja|[[ukiyo-e]]}} prints. Stickers on shrines are often pasted in very obvious, easily seen locations, but a variation on this practice is to purposely obscure the location of the senjafuda in order to protect it from exposure to wind and rain and thus prolong its presence.
== History == {{transl|ja|Senjafuda}} were first produced in the [[Heian period]] (794-1185) when shrine worshipers made pilgrimages to visits to many shrines and worship the Buddhist goddess of mercy, [[Kannon]]. They were not originally made of paper, they were first made from wooden slats that were hung from the gates of Kannon temples by nails made of bamboo. The slats were carved out with the visitors' name, area of origin and often included a prayer for a good life and afterlife.
There are two styles of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}}: the older style, {{transl|ja|daimei nosatsu}}, and the newer style, {{transl|ja|kokan nosatsu}}. {{transl|ja|Daimei nosatsu}} are basic black ink on white paper. The [[inkstick|ink]] used is so strong that after the printed {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} are placed on the shrine or temple gate, years later when the paper is peeled away, the ink remains. Therefore, many shrine {{transl|ja|[[kannushi]]}} or {{transl|ja|[[shinshoku]]}} do not like the use of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}}, as well as more modern practices, where younger {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} practitioners do not pray or buy a stamp from the shrine before applying their {{transl|ja|senjafuda}}.
[[File:Hiroshige senjafuda.jpg|thumb|upright=1.3|[[Hiroshige]] {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} depicting an exchange meeting in 1842. One of the hanging banners ironically states "Graffiti strictly forbidden".]] The later style of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} are called {{transl|ja|kokan nosatsu}} and originated in the [[Edo period]] (1603-1868). During the beginning of the Edo period, shrine pilgrimages gained popularity, beginning the tradition known as {{transl|ja|[[senjamairi]]}}, meaning "a thousand shrine visits for good luck". {{transl|ja|Kokan nosatsu senjafuda}} are a lot more colorful than {{transl|ja|saimei nosatsu}}, and have rich patterns and designs, being used more as novelty items and more like trading cards or the business cards of today. Like many things during the Edo period, {{transl|ja|kokan nosatsu senjafuda}} were regulated, with the number of colours on a person's {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} limited to their class and place in society.
Because of this, collectors who enjoyed the many designs and colors of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} began meeting to exchange them with one another; first, the meetings took place at private homes, and then later were arranged for public places like restaurants and expensive [[ochaya|tea houses]]. According to Kiritani's ''Vanishing Japan'', the oldest surviving invitation card to a {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} meeting dates back to 1799.<ref>Kiritani, Elizabeth ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=5_GGbiw1-y4C&pg=PA1&lpg=PA1&dq=vanishing+japan&source=bl&ots=vqjFnOgLhM&sig=QxOIQh0fX-spvbI0NRy4zt2f2yo&hl=ja&ei=VRqKSqb7F9KfkQW9rNgt&sa=X&oi=book_result&ct=result&resnum=1#v=onepage&q=&f=false Vanishing Japan]''</ref> Due to the growing popularity of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} meetings, the government enforced a law forbidding their trading, which did not stop the meetings from taking place. {{transl|ja|Senjafuda}} meetings continue to this day, with collectors and aficionados alike meeting to share and trade their own designs as well as admire others.
[[United States|US]] collector and Japanese anthropologist [[Frederick Starr]] was a turn-of-the-century collector and avid participant in {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} or {{transl|ja|nōsatsu-kai}} (votive slip exchange clubs), so much so that he was given the name "Dr. Ofuda". He collected tens of thousands of slips, and a fellow collector and popular art enthusiast, [[Gertrude Bass Warner]], purchased much of his collection. It currently resides at the University of Oregon Knight Library Special Collections & University Archives, part of the Gertrude Bass Warner Collection, and examples are viewable online at UO Oregon Digital.<ref>{{cite web|last1=McDowell|first1=Kevin|title=Gertrude Bass Warner Collection of Japanese Shrine and Temple Votive Slips (nōsatsu)|url=https://oregondigital.org/sets/gb-warner-nosatsu|website=Oregon Digital|publisher=University of Oregon|access-date=3 May 2017}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|last1=McDowell|first1=Kevin|title=Rare Collection: Nōsatsu Japanese Shrine and Temple Votive Slips|url=https://blogs.uoregon.edu/scua/2016/05/09/rare-collection-nosatsu-japanese-shrine-and-temple-votive-slips/|website=Upbound UO Blog|publisher=Special Collections & University Archives, University of Oregon|access-date=3 May 2017}}</ref>
== Construction == {{transl|ja|Senjafuda}} used to be made from rice paper with ink called {{transl|ja|sumi}}, and were pasted on with a rice mush. The pilgrims used to carry walking staffs for their long journeys, which doubled as an applicator for {{transl|ja|senjafuda}}. The paste was applied with something called {{transl|ja|meotobake}} - two brushes about 30 degrees apart, with a clip on the other side of the brushes, allowing {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} to be pasted in out of reach areas, leaving others to wonder exactly how they got up there.
In the present day, {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} are made from printed paper, and are rarely made traditional by wood block prints or {{transl|ja|ukiyo-e}}. Wooden slat {{transl|ja|senjafuda}}, however, are still produced, and are worn as a necklace or used for key chain and cell phone ornaments. The ones made from paper are pre-printed with common names; machines are also available that can produce custom {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} with adhesive backings.
== Famous figures == Some famous producers of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} are Hiroshige, Eisen, Kunisada, Kuniyoshi. They mainly produced {{transl|ja|senjafuda}}, due to the expensive of the {{transl|ja|ukiyo-e}} printing process.
Senrei Sekioka was one of the foremost Japanese experts of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} history; Iseman and Frederick Starr were also important members of the {{transl|ja|nosatsu-kai}} during the [[Meiji period|Meiji]] and [[Taishō period|Taishō]] eras.
== In recent years == {{transl|ja|Senjafuda}} are also sold as stickers which do not require separate paste. As stickers, they are also placed in books and on personal items for identification and decoration. A common criticism of the sticker version of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} is that they are more difficult to peel off than their original pasted ancestors, and thus can disfigure the underlying buildings when removed.
==Gallery== <gallery mode="packed" heights="230"> File:Yokai senjafuda.jpg|[[Nōsatsu]], Shōki the Demon Queller File:Senjafuda collector.jpg|Masked {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} collector tossing slips into the air File:Senjafuda foxes.jpg|thumb|Fox hairdressers File:Senjafuda fox at gates.jpg|A stone fox statue in front of a series of shrine gates at [[Anamori Inari]], Tokyo File:Senjafuda paster.jpg|Tengu Kōhei with a box for his {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} and pole-mounted brushes used for pasting </gallery>

== Construction == {{transl|ja|Senjafuda}} used to be made from rice paper with ink called {{transl|ja|sumi}}, and were pasted on with a rice mush. The pilgrims used to carry walking staffs for their long journeys, which doubled as an applicator for {{transl|ja|senjafuda}}. The paste was applied with something called {{transl|ja|meotobake}} - two brushes about 30 degrees apart, with a clip on the other side of the brushes, allowing {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} to be pasted in out of reach areas, leaving others to wonder exactly how they got up there.
In the present day, {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} are made from printed paper, and are rarely made traditional by wood block prints or {{transl|ja|ukiyo-e}}. Wooden slat {{transl|ja|senjafuda}}, however, are still produced, and are worn as a necklace or used for key chain and cell phone ornaments. The ones made from paper are pre-printed with common names; machines are also available that can produce custom {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} with adhesive backings.
== Famous figures == Some famous producers of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} are Hiroshige, Eisen, Kunisada, Kuniyoshi. They mainly produced {{transl|ja|senjafuda}}, due to the expensive of the {{transl|ja|ukiyo-e}} printing process.
Senrei Sekioka was one of the foremost Japanese experts of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} history; Iseman and Frederick Starr were also important members of the {{transl|ja|nosatsu-kai}} during the [[Meiji period|Meiji]] and [[Taishō period|Taishō]] eras.
== In recent years == {{transl|ja|Senjafuda}} are also sold as stickers which do not require separate paste. As stickers, they are also placed in books and on personal items for identification and decoration. A common criticism of the sticker version of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} is that they are more difficult to peel off than their original pasted ancestors, and thus can disfigure the underlying buildings when removed.
==Gallery== <gallery mode="packed" heights="230"> File:Yokai senjafuda.jpg|[[Nōsatsu]], Shōki the Demon Queller File:Senjafuda collector.jpg|Masked {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} collector tossing slips into the air File:Senjafuda foxes.jpg|thumb|Fox hairdressers File:Senjafuda fox at gates.jpg|A stone fox statue in front of a series of shrine gates at [[Anamori Inari]], Tokyo File:Senjafuda paster.jpg|Tengu Kōhei with a box for his {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} and pole-mounted brushes used for pasting </gallery>

{{Refimprove|date=February 2018}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} [[File:八坂神社 (4472701259).jpg|thumb|Examples on a ceiling at [[Yasaka Shrine]]]] [[File:Gifu-kegonji0059.JPG|right|thumb|300px|{{transl|ja|Senjafuda}} pasted on a shrine gate in [[Gifu, Gifu|Gifu]].]]
{{nihongo|'''Senjafuda'''|[[wiktionary:千社札|千社札]], lit. the "Thousand Shrine Tags"}} are [[votive]] slips, [[stickers]] or [[placard]]s (and [[Ofuda]]) [[Poster|posted]] on the gates or buildings of [[Shinto shrine]]s and [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] in [[Japan]]. The stickers bear the name of the worshipper, and can be purchased pre-printed with common names at temples and shrines throughout Japan, as well as at stationery stores and video game centres. {{transl|ja|Senjafuda}} were originally made from wooden slats, but have been made of paper since the [[Edo period]].
A single senjafuda measures 1.6 [[Cun (unit)|Sun]] ({{convert|58|mm|abbr=on}}) in width and 4.8 Sun ({{convert|173|mm|abbr=on}}) in height. This gives the {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} a ratio of 1:3. A frame is drawn inside this space which contains the lettering or pictures. In 1887, a measurement for this frame was also established as {{convert|48|mm|abbr=on}} wide and {{convert|144|mm|abbr=on}} tall.
Ordinarily, the designs were used to commemorate a visit to a temple or shrine and printed with simple monochromatic schemes, but eventually aesthetic sense gave way to colorful variations and designs. In the pleasure quarters of [[Kyoto]], colorful designs were employed on {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} and used in place of traditional business cards. This variation is called {{transl|ja|"hana-meishi"}} which roughly translated to "flower business card." Today, the "business card" use of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} is the most common.
{{transl|ja|Senjafuda}} were primarily printed with {{transl|ja|[[Edomoji]]}}, or Edo-period lettering styles, and pressed with the same traditional wooden boards used to produce {{transl|ja|[[ukiyo-e]]}} prints. Stickers on shrines are often pasted in very obvious, easily seen locations, but a variation on this practice is to purposely obscure the location of the senjafuda in order to protect it from exposure to wind and rain and thus prolong its presence.
== History == {{transl|ja|Senjafuda}} were first produced in the [[Heian period]] (794-1185) when shrine worshipers made pilgrimages to visits to many shrines and worship the Buddhist goddess of mercy, [[Kannon]]. They were not originally made of paper, they were first made from wooden slats that were hung from the gates of Kannon temples by nails made of bamboo. The slats were carved out with the visitors' name, area of origin and often included a prayer for a good life and afterlife.
There are two styles of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}}: the older style, {{transl|ja|daimei nosatsu}}, and the newer style, {{transl|ja|kokan nosatsu}}. {{transl|ja|Daimei nosatsu}} are basic black ink on white paper. The [[inkstick|ink]] used is so strong that after the printed {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} are placed on the shrine or temple gate, years later when the paper is peeled away, the ink remains. Therefore, many shrine {{transl|ja|[[kannushi]]}} or {{transl|ja|[[shinshoku]]}} do not like the use of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}}, as well as more modern practices, where younger {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} practitioners do not pray or buy a stamp from the shrine before applying their {{transl|ja|senjafuda}}.
[[File:Hiroshige senjafuda.jpg|thumb|upright=1.3|[[Hiroshige]] {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} depicting an exchange meeting in 1842. One of the hanging banners ironically states "Graffiti strictly forbidden".]] The later style of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} are called {{transl|ja|kokan nosatsu}} and originated in the [[Edo period]] (1603-1868). During the beginning of the Edo period, shrine pilgrimages gained popularity, beginning the tradition known as {{transl|ja|[[senjamairi]]}}, meaning "a thousand shrine visits for good luck". {{transl|ja|Kokan nosatsu senjafuda}} are a lot more colorful than {{transl|ja|saimei nosatsu}}, and have rich patterns and designs, being used more as novelty items and more like trading cards or the business cards of today. Like many things during the Edo period, {{transl|ja|kokan nosatsu senjafuda}} were regulated, with the number of colours on a person's {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} limited to their class and place in society.
Because of this, collectors who enjoyed the many designs and colors of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} began meeting to exchange them with one another; first, the meetings took place at private homes, and then later were arranged for public places like restaurants and expensive [[ochaya|tea houses]]. According to Kiritani's ''Vanishing Japan'', the oldest surviving invitation card to a {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} meeting dates back to 1799.<ref>Kiritani, Elizabeth ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=5_GGbiw1-y4C&pg=PA1&lpg=PA1&dq=vanishing+japan&source=bl&ots=vqjFnOgLhM&sig=QxOIQh0fX-spvbI0NRy4zt2f2yo&hl=ja&ei=VRqKSqb7F9KfkQW9rNgt&sa=X&oi=book_result&ct=result&resnum=1#v=onepage&q=&f=false Vanishing Japan]''</ref> Due to the growing popularity of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} meetings, the government enforced a law forbidding their trading, which did not stop the meetings from taking place. {{transl|ja|Senjafuda}} meetings continue to this day, with collectors and aficionados alike meeting to share and trade their own designs as well as admire others.
[[United States|US]] collector and Japanese anthropologist [[Frederick Starr]] was a turn-of-the-century collector and avid participant in {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} or {{transl|ja|nōsatsu-kai}} (votive slip exchange clubs), so much so that he was given the name "Dr. Ofuda". He collected tens of thousands of slips, and a fellow collector and popular art enthusiast, [[Gertrude Bass Warner]], purchased much of his collection. It currently resides at the University of Oregon Knight Library Special Collections & University Archives, part of the Gertrude Bass Warner Collection, and examples are viewable online at UO Oregon Digital.<ref>{{cite web|last1=McDowell|first1=Kevin|title=Gertrude Bass Warner Collection of Japanese Shrine and Temple Votive Slips (nōsatsu)|url=https://oregondigital.org/sets/gb-warner-nosatsu|website=Oregon Digital|publisher=University of Oregon|access-date=3 May 2017}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|last1=McDowell|first1=Kevin|title=Rare Collection: Nōsatsu Japanese Shrine and Temple Votive Slips|url=https://blogs.uoregon.edu/scua/2016/05/09/rare-collection-nosatsu-japanese-shrine-and-temple-votive-slips/|website=Upbound UO Blog|publisher=Special Collections & University Archives, University of Oregon|access-date=3 May 2017}}</ref>
== Construction == {{transl|ja|Senjafuda}} used to be made from rice paper with ink called {{transl|ja|sumi}}, and were pasted on with a rice mush. The pilgrims used to carry walking staffs for their long journeys, which doubled as an applicator for {{transl|ja|senjafuda}}. The paste was applied with something called {{transl|ja|meotobake}} - two brushes about 30 degrees apart, with a clip on the other side of the brushes, allowing {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} to be pasted in out of reach areas, leaving others to wonder exactly how they got up there.
In the present day, {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} are made from printed paper, and are rarely made traditional by wood block prints or {{transl|ja|ukiyo-e}}. Wooden slat {{transl|ja|senjafuda}}, however, are still produced, and are worn as a necklace or used for key chain and cell phone ornaments. The ones made from paper are pre-printed with common names; machines are also available that can produce custom {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} with adhesive backings.
== Famous figures == Some famous producers of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} are Hiroshige, Eisen, Kunisada, Kuniyoshi. They mainly produced {{transl|ja|senjafuda}}, due to the expensive of the {{transl|ja|ukiyo-e}} printing process.
Senrei Sekioka was one of the foremost Japanese experts of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} history; Iseman and Frederick Starr were also important members of the {{transl|ja|nosatsu-kai}} during the [[Meiji period|Meiji]] and [[Taishō period|Taishō]] eras.
== In recent years == {{transl|ja|Senjafuda}} are also sold as stickers which do not require separate paste. As stickers, they are also placed in books and on personal items for identification and decoration. A common criticism of the sticker version of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} is that they are more difficult to peel off than their original pasted ancestors, and thus can disfigure the underlying buildings when removed.

Like many things during the Edo period, {{transl|ja|kokan nosatsu senjafuda}} were regulated, with the number of colours on a person's {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} limited to their class and place in society.
Because of this, collectors who enjoyed the many designs and colors of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} began meeting to exchange them with one another; first, the meetings took place at private homes, and then later were arranged for public places like restaurants and expensive [[ochaya|tea houses]]. According to Kiritani's ''Vanishing Japan'', the oldest surviving invitation card to a {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} meeting dates back to 1799.<ref>Kiritani, Elizabeth ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=5_GGbiw1-y4C&pg=PA1&lpg=PA1&dq=vanishing+japan&source=bl&ots=vqjFnOgLhM&sig=QxOIQh0fX-spvbI0NRy4zt2f2yo&hl=ja&ei=VRqKSqb7F9KfkQW9rNgt&sa=X&oi=book_result&ct=result&resnum=1#v=onepage&q=&f=false Vanishing Japan]''</ref> Due to the growing popularity of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} meetings, the government enforced a law forbidding their trading, which did not stop the meetings from taking place. {{transl|ja|Senjafuda}} meetings continue to this day, with collectors and aficionados alike meeting to share and trade their own designs as well as admire others.
[[United States|US]] collector and Japanese anthropologist [[Frederick Starr]] was a turn-of-the-century collector and avid participant in {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} or {{transl|ja|nōsatsu-kai}} (votive slip exchange clubs), so much so that he was given the name "Dr. Ofuda". He collected tens of thousands of slips, and a fellow collector and popular art enthusiast, [[Gertrude Bass Warner]], purchased much of his collection. It currently resides at the University of Oregon Knight Library Special Collections & University Archives, part of the Gertrude Bass Warner Collection, and examples are viewable online at UO Oregon Digital.<ref>{{cite web|last1=McDowell|first1=Kevin|title=Gertrude Bass Warner Collection of Japanese Shrine and Temple Votive Slips (nōsatsu)|url=https://oregondigital.org/sets/gb-warner-nosatsu|website=Oregon Digital|publisher=University of Oregon|access-date=3 May 2017}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|last1=McDowell|first1=Kevin|title=Rare Collection: Nōsatsu Japanese Shrine and Temple Votive Slips|url=https://blogs.uoregon.edu/scua/2016/05/09/rare-collection-nosatsu-japanese-shrine-and-temple-votive-slips/|website=Upbound UO Blog|publisher=Special Collections & University Archives, University of Oregon|access-date=3 May 2017}}</ref>
== Construction == {{transl|ja|Senjafuda}} used to be made from rice paper with ink called {{transl|ja|sumi}}, and were pasted on with a rice mush. The pilgrims used to carry walking staffs for their long journeys, which doubled as an applicator for {{transl|ja|senjafuda}}. The paste was applied with something called {{transl|ja|meotobake}} - two brushes about 30 degrees apart, with a clip on the other side of the brushes, allowing {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} to be pasted in out of reach areas, leaving others to wonder exactly how they got up there.
In the present day, {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} are made from printed paper, and are rarely made traditional by wood block prints or {{transl|ja|ukiyo-e}}. Wooden slat {{transl|ja|senjafuda}}, however, are still produced, and are worn as a necklace or used for key chain and cell phone ornaments. The ones made from paper are pre-printed with common names; machines are also available that can produce custom {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} with adhesive backings.
== Famous figures == Some famous producers of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} are Hiroshige, Eisen, Kunisada, Kuniyoshi. They mainly produced {{transl|ja|senjafuda}}, due to the expensive of the {{transl|ja|ukiyo-e}} printing process.
Senrei Sekioka was one of the foremost Japanese experts of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} history; Iseman and Frederick Starr were also important members of the {{transl|ja|nosatsu-kai}} during the [[Meiji period|Meiji]] and [[Taishō period|Taishō]] eras.
== In recent years == {{transl|ja|Senjafuda}} are also sold as stickers which do not require separate paste. As stickers, they are also placed in books and on personal items for identification and decoration. A common criticism of the sticker version of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} is that they are more difficult to peel off than their original pasted ancestors, and thus can disfigure the underlying buildings when removed.
==Gallery== <gallery mode="packed" heights="230"> File:Yokai senjafuda.jpg|[[Nōsatsu]], Shōki the Demon Queller File:Senjafuda collector.jpg|Masked {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} collector tossing slips into the air File:Senjafuda foxes.jpg|thumb|Fox hairdressers File:Senjafuda fox at gates.jpg|A stone fox statue in front of a series of shrine gates at [[Anamori Inari]], Tokyo File:Senjafuda paster.jpg|Tengu Kōhei with a box for his {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} and pole-mounted brushes used for pasting </gallery>

{{Refimprove|date=February 2018}} {{Italic title}} {{for multi|votive talismans designed for the home|Ofuda|amulets sold at shrines for luck and protection|Omamori}} [[File:八坂神社 (4472701259).jpg|thumb|Examples on a ceiling at [[Yasaka Shrine]]]] [[File:Gifu-kegonji0059.JPG|right|thumb|300px|{{transl|ja|Senjafuda}} pasted on a shrine gate in [[Gifu, Gifu|Gifu]].]]
{{nihongo|'''Senjafuda'''|[[wiktionary:千社札|千社札]], lit. the "Thousand Shrine Tags"}} are [[votive]] slips, [[stickers]] or [[placard]]s [[Poster|posted]] on the gates or buildings of [[Shinto shrine]]s and [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] in [[Japan]]. Unlike {{transl|ja|[[ofuda]]}}, which bear the name of the shrine, {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} bear the name of the worshipper, and can be purchased pre-printed with common names at temples and shrines throughout Japan, as well as at stationery stores and video game centres. {{transl|ja|Senjafuda}} were originally made from wooden slats, but have been made of paper since the [[Edo period]].
A single senjafuda measures 1.6 [[Cun (unit)|Sun]] ({{convert|58|mm|abbr=on}}) in width and 4.8 Sun ({{convert|173|mm|abbr=on}}) in height. This gives the {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} a ratio of 1:3. A frame is drawn inside this space which contains the lettering or pictures. In 1887, a measurement for this frame was also established as {{convert|48|mm|abbr=on}} wide and {{convert|144|mm|abbr=on}} tall.
Ordinarily, the designs were used to commemorate a visit to a temple or shrine and printed with simple monochromatic schemes, but eventually aesthetic sense gave way to colorful variations and designs. In the pleasure quarters of [[Kyoto]], colorful designs were employed on {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} and used in place of traditional business cards. This variation is called {{transl|ja|"hana-meishi"}} which roughly translated to "flower business card." Today, the "business card" use of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} is the most common.
{{transl|ja|Senjafuda}} were primarily printed with {{transl|ja|[[Edomoji]]}}, or Edo-period lettering styles, and pressed with the same traditional wooden boards used to produce {{transl|ja|[[ukiyo-e]]}} prints. Stickers on shrines are often pasted in very obvious, easily seen locations, but a variation on this practice is to purposely obscure the location of the {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} in order to protect it from exposure to wind and rain and thus prolong its presence.
== History == {{transl|ja|Senjafuda}} were first produced in the [[Heian period]] (794-1185) when shrine worshipers made pilgrimages to visits to many shrines and worship the Buddhist goddess of mercy, [[Kannon]]. They were not originally made of paper, they were first made from wooden slats that were hung from the gates of Kannon temples by nails made of bamboo. The slats were carved out with the visitors' name, area of origin and often included a prayer for a good life and afterlife.
There are two styles of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}}: the older style, {{transl|ja|daimei nosatsu}}, and the newer style, {{transl|ja|kokan nosatsu}}. {{transl|ja|Daimei nosatsu}} are basic black ink on white paper. The [[inkstick|ink]] used is so strong that after the printed {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} are placed on the shrine or temple gate, years later when the paper is peeled away, the ink remains. Therefore, many shrine {{transl|ja|[[kannushi]]}} or {{transl|ja|[[shinshoku]]}} do not like the use of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}}, as well as more modern practices, where younger {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} practitioners do not pray or buy a stamp from the shrine before applying their {{transl|ja|senjafuda}}.
[[File:Hiroshige senjafuda.jpg|thumb|upright=1.3|[[Hiroshige]] {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} depicting an exchange meeting in 1842. One of the hanging banners ironically states "Graffiti strictly forbidden".]] The later style of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} are called {{transl|ja|kokan nosatsu}} and originated in the [[Edo period]] (1603-1868). During the beginning of the Edo period, shrine pilgrimages gained popularity, beginning the tradition known as {{transl|ja|[[senjamairi]]}}, meaning "a thousand shrine visits for good luck". {{transl|ja|Kokan nosatsu senjafuda}} are a lot more colorful than {{transl|ja|saimei nosatsu}}, and have rich patterns and designs, being used more as novelty items and more like trading cards or the business cards of today. Like many things during the Edo period, {{transl|ja|kokan nosatsu senjafuda}} were regulated, with the number of colours on a person's {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} limited to their class and place in society.
Because of this, collectors who enjoyed the many designs and colors of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} began meeting to exchange them with one another; first, the meetings took place at private homes, and then later were arranged for public places like restaurants and expensive [[ochaya|tea houses]]. According to Kiritani's ''Vanishing Japan'', the oldest surviving invitation card to a {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} meeting dates back to 1799.<ref>Kiritani, Elizabeth ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=5_GGbiw1-y4C&pg=PA1&lpg=PA1&dq=vanishing+japan&source=bl&ots=vqjFnOgLhM&sig=QxOIQh0fX-spvbI0NRy4zt2f2yo&hl=ja&ei=VRqKSqb7F9KfkQW9rNgt&sa=X&oi=book_result&ct=result&resnum=1#v=onepage&q=&f=false Vanishing Japan]''</ref> Due to the growing popularity of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} meetings, the government enforced a law forbidding their trading, which did not stop the meetings from taking place. {{transl|ja|Senjafuda}} meetings continue to this day, with collectors and aficionados alike meeting to share and trade their own designs as well as admire others.
[[United States|US]] collector and Japanese anthropologist [[Frederick Starr]] was a turn-of-the-century collector and avid participant in {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} or {{transl|ja|nōsatsu-kai}} (votive slip exchange clubs), so much so that he was given the name "Dr. Ofuda". He collected tens of thousands of slips, and a fellow collector and popular art enthusiast, [[Gertrude Bass Warner]], purchased much of his collection. It currently resides at the University of Oregon Knight Library Special Collections & University Archives, part of the Gertrude Bass Warner Collection, and examples are viewable online at UO Oregon Digital.<ref>{{cite web|last1=McDowell|first1=Kevin|title=Gertrude Bass Warner Collection of Japanese Shrine and Temple Votive Slips (nōsatsu)|url=https://oregondigital.org/sets/gb-warner-nosatsu|website=Oregon Digital|publisher=University of Oregon|access-date=3 May 2017}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|last1=McDowell|first1=Kevin|title=Rare Collection: Nōsatsu Japanese Shrine and Temple Votive Slips|url=https://blogs.uoregon.edu/scua/2016/05/09/rare-collection-nosatsu-japanese-shrine-and-temple-votive-slips/|website=Upbound UO Blog|publisher=Special Collections & University Archives, University of Oregon|access-date=3 May 2017}}</ref>
== Construction == {{transl|ja|Senjafuda}} used to be made from rice paper with ink called {{transl|ja|sumi}}, and were pasted on with a rice mush. The pilgrims used to carry walking staffs for their long journeys, which doubled as an applicator for {{transl|ja|senjafuda}}. The paste was applied with something called {{transl|ja|meotobake}} - two brushes about 30 degrees apart, with a clip on the other side of the brushes, allowing {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} to be pasted in out of reach areas, leaving others to wonder exactly how they got up there.
In the present day, {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} are made from printed paper, and are rarely made traditional by wood block prints or {{transl|ja|ukiyo-e}}. Wooden slat {{transl|ja|senjafuda}}, however, are still produced, and are worn as a necklace or used for key chain and cell phone ornaments. The ones made from paper are pre-printed with common names; machines are also available that can produce custom {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} with adhesive backings.
== Famous figures == Some famous producers of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} are Hiroshige, Eisen, Kunisada, Kuniyoshi. They mainly produced {{transl|ja|senjafuda}}, due to the expensive of the {{transl|ja|ukiyo-e}} printing process.
Senrei Sekioka was one of the foremost Japanese experts of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} history; Iseman and Frederick Starr were also important members of the {{transl|ja|nosatsu-kai}} during the [[Meiji period|Meiji]] and [[Taishō period|Taishō]] eras.
== In recent years == {{transl|ja|Senjafuda}} are also sold as stickers which do not require separate paste. As stickers, they are also placed in books and on personal items for identification and decoration. A common criticism of the sticker version of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} is that they are more difficult to peel off than their original pasted ancestors, and thus can disfigure the underlying buildings when removed.

In the pleasure quarters of [[Kyoto]], colorful designs were employed on {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} and used in place of traditional business cards. This variation is called {{transl|ja|"hana-meishi"}} which roughly translated to "flower business card." Today, the "business card" use of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} is the most common.
{{transl|ja|Senjafuda}} were primarily printed with {{transl|ja|[[Edomoji]]}}, or Edo-period lettering styles, and pressed with the same traditional wooden boards used to produce {{transl|ja|[[ukiyo-e]]}} prints. Stickers on shrines are often pasted in very obvious, easily seen locations, but a variation on this practice is to purposely obscure the location of the {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} in order to protect it from exposure to wind and rain and thus prolong its presence.
== History == {{transl|ja|Senjafuda}} were first produced in the [[Heian period]] (794-1185) when shrine worshipers made pilgrimages to visits to many shrines and worship the Buddhist goddess of mercy, [[Kannon]]. They were not originally made of paper, they were first made from wooden slats that were hung from the gates of Kannon temples by nails made of bamboo. The slats were carved out with the visitors' name, area of origin and often included a prayer for a good life and afterlife.
There are two styles of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}}: the older style, {{transl|ja|daimei nosatsu}}, and the newer style, {{transl|ja|kokan nosatsu}}. {{transl|ja|Daimei nosatsu}} are basic black ink on white paper. The [[inkstick|ink]] used is so strong that after the printed {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} are placed on the shrine or temple gate, years later when the paper is peeled away, the ink remains. Therefore, many shrine {{transl|ja|[[kannushi]]}} or {{transl|ja|[[shinshoku]]}} do not like the use of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}}, as well as more modern practices, where younger {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} practitioners do not pray or buy a stamp from the shrine before applying their {{transl|ja|senjafuda}}.
[[File:Hiroshige senjafuda.jpg|thumb|upright=1.3|[[Hiroshige]] {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} depicting an exchange meeting in 1842. One of the hanging banners ironically states "Graffiti strictly forbidden".]] The later style of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} are called {{transl|ja|kokan nosatsu}} and originated in the [[Edo period]] (1603-1868). During the beginning of the Edo period, shrine pilgrimages gained popularity, beginning the tradition known as {{transl|ja|[[senjamairi]]}}, meaning "a thousand shrine visits for good luck". {{transl|ja|Kokan nosatsu senjafuda}} are a lot more colorful than {{transl|ja|saimei nosatsu}}, and have rich patterns and designs, being used more as novelty items and more like trading cards or the business cards of today. Like many things during the Edo period, {{transl|ja|kokan nosatsu senjafuda}} were regulated, with the number of colours on a person's {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} limited to their class and place in society.
Because of this, collectors who enjoyed the many designs and colors of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} began meeting to exchange them with one another; first, the meetings took place at private homes, and then later were arranged for public places like restaurants and expensive [[ochaya|tea houses]]. According to Kiritani's ''Vanishing Japan'', the oldest surviving invitation card to a {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} meeting dates back to 1799.<ref>Kiritani, Elizabeth ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=5_GGbiw1-y4C&pg=PA1&lpg=PA1&dq=vanishing+japan&source=bl&ots=vqjFnOgLhM&sig=QxOIQh0fX-spvbI0NRy4zt2f2yo&hl=ja&ei=VRqKSqb7F9KfkQW9rNgt&sa=X&oi=book_result&ct=result&resnum=1#v=onepage&q=&f=false Vanishing Japan]''</ref> Due to the growing popularity of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} meetings, the government enforced a law forbidding their trading, which did not stop the meetings from taking place. {{transl|ja|Senjafuda}} meetings continue to this day, with collectors and aficionados alike meeting to share and trade their own designs as well as admire others.
[[United States|US]] collector and Japanese anthropologist [[Frederick Starr]] was a turn-of-the-century collector and avid participant in {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} or {{transl|ja|nōsatsu-kai}} (votive slip exchange clubs), so much so that he was given the name "Dr. Ofuda". He collected tens of thousands of slips, and a fellow collector and popular art enthusiast, [[Gertrude Bass Warner]], purchased much of his collection. It currently resides at the University of Oregon Knight Library Special Collections & University Archives, part of the Gertrude Bass Warner Collection, and examples are viewable online at UO Oregon Digital.<ref>{{cite web|last1=McDowell|first1=Kevin|title=Gertrude Bass Warner Collection of Japanese Shrine and Temple Votive Slips (nōsatsu)|url=https://oregondigital.org/sets/gb-warner-nosatsu|website=Oregon Digital|publisher=University of Oregon|access-date=3 May 2017}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|last1=McDowell|first1=Kevin|title=Rare Collection: Nōsatsu Japanese Shrine and Temple Votive Slips|url=https://blogs.uoregon.edu/scua/2016/05/09/rare-collection-nosatsu-japanese-shrine-and-temple-votive-slips/|website=Upbound UO Blog|publisher=Special Collections & University Archives, University of Oregon|access-date=3 May 2017}}</ref>
== Construction == {{transl|ja|Senjafuda}} used to be made from rice paper with ink called {{transl|ja|sumi}}, and were pasted on with a rice mush. The pilgrims used to carry walking staffs for their long journeys, which doubled as an applicator for {{transl|ja|senjafuda}}. The paste was applied with something called {{transl|ja|meotobake}} - two brushes about 30 degrees apart, with a clip on the other side of the brushes, allowing {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} to be pasted in out of reach areas, leaving others to wonder exactly how they got up there.
In the present day, {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} are made from printed paper, and are rarely made traditional by wood block prints or {{transl|ja|ukiyo-e}}. Wooden slat {{transl|ja|senjafuda}}, however, are still produced, and are worn as a necklace or used for key chain and cell phone ornaments. The ones made from paper are pre-printed with common names; machines are also available that can produce custom {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} with adhesive backings.
== Famous figures == Some famous producers of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} are Hiroshige, Eisen, Kunisada, Kuniyoshi. They mainly produced {{transl|ja|senjafuda}}, due to the expensive of the {{transl|ja|ukiyo-e}} printing process.
Senrei Sekioka was one of the foremost Japanese experts of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} history; Iseman and Frederick Starr were also important members of the {{transl|ja|nosatsu-kai}} during the [[Meiji period|Meiji]] and [[Taishō period|Taishō]] eras.
== In recent years == {{transl|ja|Senjafuda}} are also sold as stickers which do not require separate paste. As stickers, they are also placed in books and on personal items for identification and decoration. A common criticism of the sticker version of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} is that they are more difficult to peel off than their original pasted ancestors, and thus can disfigure the underlying buildings when removed.
==Gallery== <gallery mode="packed" heights="230"> File:Yokai senjafuda.jpg|[[Nōsatsu]], Shōki the Demon Queller File:Senjafuda collector.jpg|Masked {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} collector tossing slips into the air File:Senjafuda foxes.jpg|thumb|Fox hairdressers File:Senjafuda fox at gates.jpg|A stone fox statue in front of a series of shrine gates at [[Anamori Inari]], Tokyo File:Senjafuda paster.jpg|Tengu Kōhei with a box for his {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} and pole-mounted brushes used for pasting </gallery>

{{Refimprove|date=February 2018}} {{Italic title}} {{for multi|votive talismans designed for the home|Ofuda|amulets sold at shrines for luck and protection|Omamori}} [[File:八坂神社 (4472701259).jpg|thumb|Examples on a ceiling at [[Yasaka Shrine]]]] [[File:Gifu-kegonji0059.JPG|right|thumb|300px|{{transl|ja|Senjafuda}} pasted on a shrine gate in [[Gifu, Gifu|Gifu]].]]
{{nihongo|'''Senjafuda'''|[[wiktionary:千社札|千社札]], lit. the "Thousand Shrine Tags"}} are [[votive]] slips, [[stickers]] or [[placard]]s [[Poster|posted]] on the gates or buildings of [[Shinto shrine]]s and [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] in [[Japan]]. Unlike {{transl|ja|[[ofuda]]}}, which bear the name of the shrine, {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} bear the name of the worshipper, and can be purchased pre-printed with common names at temples and shrines throughout Japan, as well as at stationery stores and video game centres. {{transl|ja|Senjafuda}} were originally made from wooden slats, but have been made of paper since the [[Edo period]].
A single senjafuda measures 1.6 {{transl|ja|[[Cun (unit)|sun]]}} ({{convert|58|mm|abbr=on}}) in width and 4.8 {{transl|ja|sun}} ({{convert|173|mm|abbr=on}}) in height. This gives the {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} a ratio of 1:3. A frame is drawn inside this space which contains the lettering or pictures. In 1887, a measurement for this frame was also established as {{convert|48|mm|abbr=on}} wide and {{convert|144|mm|abbr=on}} tall.
Ordinarily, the designs were used to commemorate a visit to a temple or shrine and printed with simple monochromatic schemes, but eventually aesthetic sense gave way to colorful variations and designs. In the pleasure quarters of [[Kyoto]], colorful designs were employed on {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} and used in place of traditional business cards. {{ukiyo-e}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}}
[[Category:Japanese culture]] [[Category:Buddhism in the Edo period]] [[Category:Ukiyo-e]] [[Category:Buddhist religious objects]] [[Category:Shinto cult objects]] [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]]

{{Refimprove|date=February 2018}} {{Italic title}} {{for multi|votive talismans designed for the home|Ofuda|amulets sold at shrines for luck and protection|Omamori|paper fortunes received by making a small offering|O-mikuji}} [[File:八坂神社 (4472701259).jpg|thumb|Examples on a ceiling at [[Yasaka Shrine]]]] [[File:Gifu-kegonji0059.JPG|right|thumb|300px|{{transl|ja|Senjafuda}} pasted on a shrine gate in [[Gifu, Gifu|Gifu]].]]
{{nihongo|'''Senjafuda'''|[[wiktionary:千社札|千社札]], lit. the "Thousand Shrine Tags"}} are [[votive]] slips, [[stickers]] or [[placard]]s [[Poster|posted]] on the gates or buildings of [[Shinto shrine]]s and [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] in [[Japan]]. Unlike {{transl|ja|[[ofuda]]}}, which bear the name of the shrine, {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} bear the name of the worshipper, and can be purchased pre-printed with common names at temples and shrines throughout Japan, as well as at stationery stores and video game centres. {{transl|ja|Senjafuda}} were originally made from wooden slats, but have been made of paper since the [[Edo period]].
A single senjafuda measures 1.6 {{transl|ja|[[Cun (unit)|sun]]}} ({{convert|58|mm|abbr=on}}) in width and 4.8 {{transl|ja|sun}} ({{convert|173|mm|abbr=on}}) in height. This gives the {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} a ratio of 1:3. A frame is drawn inside this space which contains the lettering or pictures. In 1887, a measurement for this frame was also established as {{convert|48|mm|abbr=on}} wide and {{convert|144|mm|abbr=on}} tall.
Ordinarily, the designs were used to commemorate a visit to a temple or shrine and printed with simple monochromatic schemes, but eventually aesthetic sense gave way to colorful variations and designs. In the pleasure quarters of [[Kyoto]], colorful designs were employed on {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} and used in place of traditional business cards. This variation is called {{transl|ja|"hana-meishi"}} which roughly translated to "flower business card." Today, the "business card" use of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} is the most common.
{{transl|ja|Senjafuda}} were primarily printed with {{transl|ja|[[Edomoji]]}}, or Edo-period lettering styles, and pressed with the same traditional wooden boards used to produce {{transl|ja|[[ukiyo-e]]}} prints. Stickers on shrines are often pasted in very obvious, easily seen locations, but a variation on this practice is to purposely obscure the location of the {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} in order to protect it from exposure to wind and rain and thus prolong its presence.
== History == {{transl|ja|Senjafuda}} were first produced in the [[Heian period]] (794-1185) when shrine worshipers made pilgrimages to visits to many shrines and worship the Buddhist goddess of mercy, [[Kannon]]. They were not originally made of paper, they were first made from wooden slats that were hung from the gates of Kannon temples by nails made of bamboo. The slats were carved out with the visitors' name, area of origin and often included a prayer for a good life and afterlife.
There are two styles of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}}: the older style, {{transl|ja|daimei nosatsu}}, and the newer style, {{transl|ja|kokan nosatsu}}. {{transl|ja|Daimei nosatsu}} are basic black ink on white paper. The [[inkstick|ink]] used is so strong that after the printed {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} are placed on the shrine or temple gate, years later when the paper is peeled away, the ink remains. Therefore, many shrine {{transl|ja|[[kannushi]]}} or {{transl|ja|[[shinshoku]]}} do not like the use of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}}, as well as more modern practices, where younger {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} practitioners do not pray or buy a stamp from the shrine before applying their {{transl|ja|senjafuda}}.
[[File:Hiroshige senjafuda.jpg|thumb|upright=1.3|[[Hiroshige]] {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} depicting an exchange meeting in 1842. One of the hanging banners ironically states "Graffiti strictly forbidden".]] The later style of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} are called {{transl|ja|kokan nosatsu}} and originated in the [[Edo period]] (1603-1868). During the beginning of the Edo period, shrine pilgrimages gained popularity, beginning the tradition known as {{transl|ja|[[senjamairi]]}}, meaning "a thousand shrine visits for good luck". {{transl|ja|Kokan nosatsu senjafuda}} are a lot more colorful than {{transl|ja|saimei nosatsu}}, and have rich patterns and designs, being used more as novelty items and more like trading cards or the business cards of today. Like many things during the Edo period, {{transl|ja|kokan nosatsu senjafuda}} were regulated, with the number of colours on a person's {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} limited to their class and place in society.
Because of this, collectors who enjoyed the many designs and colors of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} began meeting to exchange them with one another; first, the meetings took place at private homes, and then later were arranged for public places like restaurants and expensive [[ochaya|tea houses]]. According to Kiritani's ''Vanishing Japan'', the oldest surviving invitation card to a {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} meeting dates back to 1799.<ref>Kiritani, Elizabeth ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=5_GGbiw1-y4C&pg=PA1&lpg=PA1&dq=vanishing+japan&source=bl&ots=vqjFnOgLhM&sig=QxOIQh0fX-spvbI0NRy4zt2f2yo&hl=ja&ei=VRqKSqb7F9KfkQW9rNgt&sa=X&oi=book_result&ct=result&resnum=1#v=onepage&q=&f=false Vanishing Japan]''</ref> Due to the growing popularity of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} meetings, the government enforced a law forbidding their trading, which did not stop the meetings from taking place. {{transl|ja|Senjafuda}} meetings continue to this day, with collectors and aficionados alike meeting to share and trade their own designs as well as admire others.
[[United States|US]] collector and Japanese anthropologist [[Frederick Starr]] was a turn-of-the-century collector and avid participant in {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} or {{transl|ja|nōsatsu-kai}} (votive slip exchange clubs), so much so that he was given the name "Dr. Ofuda". He collected tens of thousands of slips, and a fellow collector and popular art enthusiast, [[Gertrude Bass Warner]], purchased much of his collection. It currently resides at the University of Oregon Knight Library Special Collections & University Archives, part of the Gertrude Bass Warner Collection, and examples are viewable online at UO Oregon Digital.<ref>{{cite web|last1=McDowell|first1=Kevin|title=Gertrude Bass Warner Collection of Japanese Shrine and Temple Votive Slips (nōsatsu)|url=https://oregondigital.org/sets/gb-warner-nosatsu|website=Oregon Digital|publisher=University of Oregon|access-date=3 May 2017}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|last1=McDowell|first1=Kevin|title=Rare Collection: Nōsatsu Japanese Shrine and Temple Votive Slips|url=https://blogs.uoregon.edu/scua/2016/05/09/rare-collection-nosatsu-japanese-shrine-and-temple-votive-slips/|website=Upbound UO Blog|publisher=Special Collections & University Archives, University of Oregon|access-date=3 May 2017}}</ref>
== Construction == {{transl|ja|Senjafuda}} used to be made from [[rice paper]] with ink called {{transl|ja|sumi}}, and were pasted on with a starchy rice paste. The pilgrims used to carry walking staffs for their long journeys, which doubled as an applicator for {{transl|ja|senjafuda}}. The paste was applied with something called {{transl|ja|meotobake}} - two brushes about 30 degrees apart, with a clip on the other side of the brushes, allowing {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} to be pasted in out of reach areas, leaving others to wonder exactly how they got up there.
In the present day, {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} are made from printed paper, and are rarely made traditionally through wood block printing. Wooden slat {{transl|ja|senjafuda}}, however, are still produced, and are worn as a necklace or used for key chain and cell phone ornaments. The ones made from paper are pre-printed with common names; machines are also available that can produce custom {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} with adhesive backings.
== Famous figures == Some famous producers of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} are Hiroshige, Eisen, Kunisada, Kuniyoshi. They mainly produced {{transl|ja|senjafuda}}, due to the expensive of the [[ukiyo-e]] printing process.
Senrei Sekioka was one of the foremost Japanese experts of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} history; Iseman and Frederick Starr were also important members of the {{transl|ja|nosatsu-kai}} during the [[Meiji period|Meiji]] and [[Taishō period|Taishō]] eras.
== In recent years == {{transl|ja|Senjafuda}} are also sold as stickers which do not require separate paste. As stickers, they are also placed in books and on personal items for identification and decoration. A common criticism of the sticker version of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} is that they are more difficult to peel off than their original pasted ancestors, and thus can disfigure the underlying buildings when removed.
==Gallery== <gallery mode="packed" heights="230"> File:Yokai senjafuda.jpg|[[Nōsatsu]], Shōki the Demon Queller File:Senjafuda collector.jpg|Masked {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} collector tossing slips into the air File:Senjafuda foxes.jpg|thumb|Fox hairdressers File:Senjafuda fox at gates.jpg|A stone fox statue in front of a series of shrine gates at [[Anamori Inari]], Tokyo File:Senjafuda paster.jpg|Tengu Kōhei with a box for his {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} and pole-mounted brushes used for pasting </gallery>

He collected tens of thousands of slips, and a fellow collector and popular art enthusiast, [[Gertrude Bass Warner]], purchased much of his collection. It currently resides at the University of Oregon Knight Library Special Collections & University Archives, part of the Gertrude Bass Warner Collection, and examples are viewable online at UO Oregon Digital.<ref>{{cite web|last1=McDowell|first1=Kevin|title=Gertrude Bass Warner Collection of Japanese Shrine and Temple Votive Slips (nōsatsu)|url=https://oregondigital.org/sets/gb-warner-nosatsu|website=Oregon Digital|publisher=University of Oregon|access-date=3 May 2017}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|last1=McDowell|first1=Kevin|title=Rare Collection: Nōsatsu Japanese Shrine and Temple Votive Slips|url=https://blogs.uoregon.edu/scua/2016/05/09/rare-collection-nosatsu-japanese-shrine-and-temple-votive-slips/|website=Upbound UO Blog|publisher=Special Collections & University Archives, University of Oregon|access-date=3 May 2017}}</ref>
== Construction == {{transl|ja|Senjafuda}} used to be made from [[rice paper]] with ink called {{transl|ja|sumi}}, and were pasted on with a starchy rice paste. The pilgrims used to carry walking staffs for their long journeys, which doubled as an applicator for {{transl|ja|senjafuda}}. The paste was applied with something called {{transl|ja|meotobake}} - two brushes about 30 degrees apart, with a clip on the other side of the brushes, allowing {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} to be pasted in out of reach areas, leaving others to wonder exactly how they got up there.
In the present day, {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} are made from printed paper, and are rarely made traditionally through wood block printing. Wooden slat {{transl|ja|senjafuda}}, however, are still produced, and are worn as a necklace or used for key chain and cell phone ornaments. The ones made from paper are pre-printed with common names; machines are also available that can produce custom {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} with adhesive backings.
== Famous figures == Some famous producers of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} are Hiroshige, Eisen, Kunisada, Kuniyoshi. They mainly produced {{transl|ja|senjafuda}}, due to the expensive of the [[ukiyo-e]] printing process.
Senrei Sekioka was one of the foremost Japanese experts of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} history; Iseman and Frederick Starr were also important members of the {{transl|ja|nosatsu-kai}} during the [[Meiji period|Meiji]] and [[Taishō period|Taishō]] eras.
== In recent years == {{transl|ja|Senjafuda}} are also sold as stickers which do not require separate paste. As stickers, they are also placed in books and on personal items for identification and decoration. A common criticism of the sticker version of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} is that they are more difficult to peel off than their original pasted ancestors, and thus can disfigure the underlying buildings when removed.
==Gallery== <gallery mode="packed" heights="230"> File:Yokai senjafuda.jpg|[[Nōsatsu]], Shōki the Demon Queller File:Senjafuda collector.jpg|Masked {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} collector tossing slips into the air File:Senjafuda foxes.jpg|thumb|Fox hairdressers File:Senjafuda fox at gates.jpg|A stone fox statue in front of a series of shrine gates at [[Anamori Inari]], Tokyo File:Senjafuda paster.jpg|Tengu Kōhei with a box for his {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} and pole-mounted brushes used for pasting </gallery>

==References== *Gordenker, Alice, "[http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/ek20101118wh.html So, What the Heck is That? Shrine tags]", ''[[Japan Times]]'', 18 November 2010, p. 13.

{{Refimprove|date=February 2018}} {{Italic title}} {{for multi|votive talismans designed for the home|Ofuda|amulets sold at shrines for luck and protection|Omamori|paper fortunes received by making a small offering|O-mikuji|wooden plaques representing prayers and wishes|Ema (Shinto)}} [[File:八坂神社 (4472701259).jpg|thumb|Examples on a ceiling at [[Yasaka Shrine]]]] [[File:Gifu-kegonji0059.JPG|right|thumb|300px|{{transl|ja|Senjafuda}} pasted on a shrine gate in [[Gifu, Gifu|Gifu]].]]
{{nihongo|'''Senjafuda'''|[[wiktionary:千社札|千社札]], lit. the "Thousand Shrine Tags"}} are [[votive]] slips, [[stickers]] or [[placard]]s [[Poster|posted]] on the gates or buildings of [[Shinto shrine]]s and [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] in [[Japan]]. Unlike {{transl|ja|[[ofuda]]}}, which bear the name of the shrine, {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} bear the name of the worshipper, and can be purchased pre-printed with common names at temples and shrines throughout Japan, as well as at stationery stores and video game centres. {{transl|ja|Senjafuda}} were originally made from wooden slats, but have been made of paper since the [[Edo period]].
A single senjafuda measures 1.6 {{transl|ja|[[Cun (unit)|sun]]}} ({{convert|58|mm|abbr=on}}) in width and 4.8 {{transl|ja|sun}} ({{convert|173|mm|abbr=on}}) in height. This gives the {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} a ratio of 1:3. A frame is drawn inside this space which contains the lettering or pictures. In 1887, a measurement for this frame was also established as {{convert|48|mm|abbr=on}} wide and {{convert|144|mm|abbr=on}} tall.
Ordinarily, the designs were used to commemorate a visit to a temple or shrine and printed with simple monochromatic schemes, but eventually aesthetic sense gave way to colorful variations and designs. In the pleasure quarters of [[Kyoto]], colorful designs were employed on {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} and used in place of traditional business cards. This variation is called {{transl|ja|"hana-meishi"}} which roughly translated to "flower business card." Today, the "business card" use of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} is the most common.
{{transl|ja|Senjafuda}} were primarily printed with {{transl|ja|[[Edomoji]]}}, or Edo-period lettering styles, and pressed with the same traditional wooden boards used to produce {{transl|ja|[[ukiyo-e]]}} prints. Stickers on shrines are often pasted in very obvious, easily seen locations, but a variation on this practice is to purposely obscure the location of the {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} in order to protect it from exposure to wind and rain and thus prolong its presence.
== History == {{transl|ja|Senjafuda}} were first produced in the [[Heian period]] (794-1185) when shrine worshipers made pilgrimages to visits to many shrines and worship the Buddhist goddess of mercy, [[Kannon]]. They were not originally made of paper, they were first made from wooden slats that were hung from the gates of Kannon temples by nails made of bamboo. The slats were carved out with the visitors' name, area of origin and often included a prayer for a good life and afterlife.
There are two styles of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}}: the older style, {{transl|ja|daimei nosatsu}}, and the newer style, {{transl|ja|kokan nosatsu}}. {{transl|ja|Daimei nosatsu}} are basic black ink on white paper. The [[inkstick|ink]] used is so strong that after the printed {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} are placed on the shrine or temple gate, years later when the paper is peeled away, the ink remains. Therefore, many shrine {{transl|ja|[[kannushi]]}} or {{transl|ja|[[shinshoku]]}} do not like the use of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}}, as well as more modern practices, where younger {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} practitioners do not pray or buy a stamp from the shrine before applying their {{transl|ja|senjafuda}}.
[[File:Hiroshige senjafuda.jpg|thumb|upright=1.3|[[Hiroshige]] {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} depicting an exchange meeting in 1842. One of the hanging banners ironically states "Graffiti strictly forbidden".]] The later style of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} are called {{transl|ja|kokan nosatsu}} and originated in the [[Edo period]] (1603-1868). During the beginning of the Edo period, shrine pilgrimages gained popularity, beginning the tradition known as {{transl|ja|[[senjamairi]]}}, meaning "a thousand shrine visits for good luck". {{transl|ja|Kokan nosatsu senjafuda}} are a lot more colorful than {{transl|ja|saimei nosatsu}}, and have rich patterns and designs, being used more as novelty items and more like trading cards or the business cards of today. Like many things during the Edo period, {{transl|ja|kokan nosatsu senjafuda}} were regulated, with the number of colours on a person's {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} limited to their class and place in society.
Because of this, collectors who enjoyed the many designs and colors of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} began meeting to exchange them with one another; first, the meetings took place at private homes, and then later were arranged for public places like restaurants and expensive [[ochaya|tea houses]]. According to Kiritani's ''Vanishing Japan'', the oldest surviving invitation card to a {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} meeting dates back to 1799.<ref>Kiritani, Elizabeth ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=5_GGbiw1-y4C&pg=PA1&lpg=PA1&dq=vanishing+japan&source=bl&ots=vqjFnOgLhM&sig=QxOIQh0fX-spvbI0NRy4zt2f2yo&hl=ja&ei=VRqKSqb7F9KfkQW9rNgt&sa=X&oi=book_result&ct=result&resnum=1#v=onepage&q=&f=false Vanishing Japan]''</ref> Due to the growing popularity of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} meetings, the government enforced a law forbidding their trading, which did not stop the meetings from taking place. {{transl|ja|Senjafuda}} meetings continue to this day, with collectors and aficionados alike meeting to share and trade their own designs as well as admire others.
[[United States|US]] collector and Japanese anthropologist [[Frederick Starr]] was a turn-of-the-century collector and avid participant in {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} or {{transl|ja|nōsatsu-kai}} (votive slip exchange clubs), so much so that he was given the name "Dr. Ofuda". He collected tens of thousands of slips, and a fellow collector and popular art enthusiast, [[Gertrude Bass Warner]], purchased much of his collection. It currently resides at the University of Oregon Knight Library Special Collections & University Archives, part of the Gertrude Bass Warner Collection, and examples are viewable online at UO Oregon Digital.<ref>{{cite web|last1=McDowell|first1=Kevin|title=Gertrude Bass Warner Collection of Japanese Shrine and Temple Votive Slips (nōsatsu)|url=https://oregondigital.org/sets/gb-warner-nosatsu|website=Oregon Digital|publisher=University of Oregon|access-date=3 May 2017}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|last1=McDowell|first1=Kevin|title=Rare Collection: Nōsatsu Japanese Shrine and Temple Votive Slips|url=https://blogs.uoregon.edu/scua/2016/05/09/rare-collection-nosatsu-japanese-shrine-and-temple-votive-slips/|website=Upbound UO Blog|publisher=Special Collections & University Archives, University of Oregon|access-date=3 May 2017}}</ref>
== Construction == {{transl|ja|Senjafuda}} used to be made from [[rice paper]] with ink called {{transl|ja|sumi}}, and were pasted on with a starchy rice paste. The pilgrims used to carry walking staffs for their long journeys, which doubled as an applicator for {{transl|ja|senjafuda}}. The paste was applied with something called {{transl|ja|meotobake}} - two brushes about 30 degrees apart, with a clip on the other side of the brushes, allowing {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} to be pasted in out of reach areas, leaving others to wonder exactly how they got up there.
In the present day, {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} are made from printed paper, and are rarely made traditionally through wood block printing. Wooden slat {{transl|ja|senjafuda}}, however, are still produced, and are worn as a necklace or used for key chain and cell phone ornaments. The ones made from paper are pre-printed with common names; machines are also available that can produce custom {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} with adhesive backings.
== Famous figures == Some famous producers of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} are Hiroshige, Eisen, Kunisada, Kuniyoshi. They mainly produced {{transl|ja|senjafuda}}, due to the expensive of the [[ukiyo-e]] printing process.
Senrei Sekioka was one of the foremost Japanese experts of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} history; Iseman and Frederick Starr were also important members of the {{transl|ja|nosatsu-kai}} during the [[Meiji period|Meiji]] and [[Taishō period|Taishō]] eras.
== In recent years == {{transl|ja|Senjafuda}} are also sold as stickers which do not require separate paste. As stickers, they are also placed in books and on personal items for identification and decoration. A common criticism of the sticker version of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} is that they are more difficult to peel off than their original pasted ancestors, and thus can disfigure the underlying buildings when removed.
==Gallery== <gallery mode="packed" heights="230"> File:Yokai senjafuda.jpg|[[Nōsatsu]], Shōki the Demon Queller File:Senjafuda collector.jpg|Masked {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} collector tossing slips into the air File:Senjafuda foxes.jpg|thumb|Fox hairdressers File:Senjafuda fox at gates.jpg|A stone fox statue in front of a series of shrine gates at [[Anamori Inari]], Tokyo File:Senjafuda paster.jpg|Tengu Kōhei with a box for his {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} and pole-mounted brushes used for pasting </gallery>
==Notes== {{Reflist}}
==References== *Gordenker, Alice, "[http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/ek20101118wh.html So, What the Heck is That? Shrine tags]", ''[[Japan Times]]'', 18 November 2010, p. 13.

They were not originally made of paper, they were first made from wooden slats that were hung from the gates of Kannon temples by nails made of bamboo. The slats were carved out with the visitors' name, area of origin and often included a prayer for a good life and afterlife.
There are two styles of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}}: the older style, {{transl|ja|daimei nosatsu}}, and the newer style, {{transl|ja|kokan nosatsu}}. {{transl|ja|Daimei nosatsu}} are basic black ink on white paper. The [[inkstick|ink]] used is so strong that after the printed {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} are placed on the shrine or temple gate, years later when the paper is peeled away, the ink remains. Therefore, many shrine {{transl|ja|[[kannushi]]}} or {{transl|ja|[[shinshoku]]}} do not like the use of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}}, as well as more modern practices, where younger {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} practitioners do not pray or buy a stamp from the shrine before applying their {{transl|ja|senjafuda}}.
[[File:Hiroshige senjafuda.jpg|thumb|upright=1.3|[[Hiroshige]] {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} depicting an exchange meeting in 1842. One of the hanging banners ironically states "Graffiti strictly forbidden".]] The later style of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} are called {{transl|ja|kokan nosatsu}} and originated in the [[Edo period]] (1603-1868). During the beginning of the Edo period, shrine pilgrimages gained popularity, beginning the tradition known as {{transl|ja|[[senjamairi]]}}, meaning "a thousand shrine visits for good luck". {{transl|ja|Kokan nosatsu senjafuda}} are a lot more colorful than {{transl|ja|saimei nosatsu}}, and have rich patterns and designs, being used more as novelty items and more like trading cards or the business cards of today. Like many things during the Edo period, {{transl|ja|kokan nosatsu senjafuda}} were regulated, with the number of colours on a person's {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} limited to their class and place in society.
Because of this, collectors who enjoyed the many designs and colors of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} began meeting to exchange them with one another; first, the meetings took place at private homes, and then later were arranged for public places like restaurants and expensive [[ochaya|tea houses]]. According to Kiritani's ''Vanishing Japan'', the oldest surviving invitation card to a {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} meeting dates back to 1799.<ref>Kiritani, Elizabeth ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=5_GGbiw1-y4C&pg=PA1&lpg=PA1&dq=vanishing+japan&source=bl&ots=vqjFnOgLhM&sig=QxOIQh0fX-spvbI0NRy4zt2f2yo&hl=ja&ei=VRqKSqb7F9KfkQW9rNgt&sa=X&oi=book_result&ct=result&resnum=1#v=onepage&q=&f=false Vanishing Japan]''</ref> Due to the growing popularity of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} meetings, the government enforced a law forbidding their trading, which did not stop the meetings from taking place. {{transl|ja|Senjafuda}} meetings continue to this day, with collectors and aficionados alike meeting to share and trade their own designs as well as admire others.
[[United States|US]] collector and Japanese anthropologist [[Frederick Starr]] was a turn-of-the-century collector and avid participant in {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} or {{transl|ja|nōsatsu-kai}} (votive slip exchange clubs), so much so that he was given the name "Dr. Ofuda". He collected tens of thousands of slips, and a fellow collector and popular art enthusiast, [[Gertrude Bass Warner]], purchased much of his collection. It currently resides at the University of Oregon Knight Library Special Collections & University Archives, part of the Gertrude Bass Warner Collection, and examples are viewable online at UO Oregon Digital.<ref>{{cite web|last1=McDowell|first1=Kevin|title=Gertrude Bass Warner Collection of Japanese Shrine and Temple Votive Slips (nōsatsu)|url=https://oregondigital.org/sets/gb-warner-nosatsu|website=Oregon Digital|publisher=University of Oregon|access-date=3 May 2017}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|last1=McDowell|first1=Kevin|title=Rare Collection: Nōsatsu Japanese Shrine and Temple Votive Slips|url=https://blogs.uoregon.edu/scua/2016/05/09/rare-collection-nosatsu-japanese-shrine-and-temple-votive-slips/|website=Upbound UO Blog|publisher=Special Collections & University Archives, University of Oregon|access-date=3 May 2017}}</ref>
== Construction == {{transl|ja|Senjafuda}} used to be made from [[rice paper]] with ink called {{transl|ja|sumi}}, and were pasted on with a starchy rice paste. The pilgrims used to carry walking staffs for their long journeys, which doubled as an applicator for {{transl|ja|senjafuda}}. The paste was applied with something called {{transl|ja|meotobake}} - two brushes about 30 degrees apart, with a clip on the other side of the brushes, allowing {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} to be pasted in out of reach areas, leaving others to wonder exactly how they got up there.
In the present day, {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} are made from printed paper, and are rarely made traditionally through wood block printing. Wooden slat {{transl|ja|senjafuda}}, however, are still produced, and are worn as a necklace or used for key chain and cell phone ornaments. The ones made from paper are pre-printed with common names; machines are also available that can produce custom {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} with adhesive backings.
== Famous figures == Some famous producers of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} are Hiroshige, Eisen, Kunisada, Kuniyoshi. They mainly produced {{transl|ja|senjafuda}}, due to the expensive of the [[ukiyo-e]] printing process.
Senrei Sekioka was one of the foremost Japanese experts of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} history; Iseman and Frederick Starr were also important members of the {{transl|ja|nosatsu-kai}} during the [[Meiji period|Meiji]] and [[Taishō period|Taishō]] eras.
== In recent years == {{transl|ja|Senjafuda}} are also sold as stickers which do not require separate paste. As stickers, they are also placed in books and on personal items for identification and decoration. A common criticism of the sticker version of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} is that they are more difficult to peel off than their original pasted ancestors, and thus can disfigure the underlying buildings when removed.
==Gallery== <gallery mode="packed" heights="230"> File:Yokai senjafuda.jpg|[[Nōsatsu]], Shōki the Demon Queller File:Senjafuda collector.jpg|Masked {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} collector tossing slips into the air File:Senjafuda foxes.jpg|thumb|Fox hairdressers File:Senjafuda fox at gates.jpg|A stone fox statue in front of a series of shrine gates at [[Anamori Inari]], Tokyo File:Senjafuda paster.jpg|Tengu Kōhei with a box for his {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} and pole-mounted brushes used for pasting </gallery>

{{Refimprove|date=February 2018}} {{Italic title}} {{for multi|votive talismans designed for the home|Ofuda|amulets sold at shrines for luck and protection|Omamori|paper fortunes received by making a small offering|O-mikuji|wooden plaques representing prayers and wishes|Ema (Shinto)}} [[File:八坂神社 (4472701259).jpg|thumb|Examples on a ceiling at [[Yasaka Shrine]]]] [[File:Gifu-kegonji0059.JPG|right|thumb|300px|{{transliteration|ja|Senjafuda}} pasted on a shrine gate in [[Gifu, Gifu|Gifu]].]]
{{nihongo3|{{lit|thousand-shrine tags}}|[[wiktionary:千社札|千社札]]|'''Senjafuda'''}} are [[votive]] slips, [[stickers]] or [[placard]]s [[Poster|posted]] on the gates or buildings of [[Shinto shrine]]s and [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] in [[Japan]]. Unlike {{transliteration|ja|[[ofuda]]}}, which bear the name of the shrine, {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} bear the name of the worshipper, and can be purchased pre-printed with common names at temples and shrines throughout Japan, as well as at stationery stores and video game centres. {{transliteration|ja|Senjafuda}} were originally made from wooden slats, but have been made of paper since the [[Edo period]].
A single senjafuda measures 1.6 {{transliteration|ja|[[Cun (unit)|sun]]}} ({{cvt|58|mm}}) in width and 4.8 {{transl|ja|sun}} ({{cvt|173|mm}}) in height. This gives the {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} a ratio of 1:3. A frame is drawn inside this space which contains the lettering or pictures. In 1887, a measurement for this frame was also established as {{cvt|48|mm}} wide and {{cvt|144|mm}} tall.
Ordinarily, the designs were used to commemorate a visit to a temple or shrine and printed with simple monochromatic schemes, but eventually aesthetic sense gave way to colorful variations and designs. In the pleasure quarters of [[Kyoto]], colorful designs were employed on {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} and used in place of traditional business cards. This variation is called {{transliteration|ja|hana-meishi}} which roughly translated to "flower business card." Today, the "business card" use of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} is the most common.
{{transliteration|ja|Senjafuda}} were primarily printed with {{transliteration|ja|[[Edomoji]]}}, or Edo-period lettering styles, and pressed with the same traditional wooden boards used to produce [[ukiyo-e]] prints. Stickers on shrines are often pasted in very obvious, easily seen locations, but a variation on this practice is to purposely obscure the location of the {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} in order to protect it from exposure to wind and rain and thus prolong its presence.
== History == {{transl|ja|Senjafuda}} were first produced in the [[Heian period]] (794-1185) when shrine worshipers made pilgrimages to visits to many shrines and worship the Buddhist goddess of mercy, [[Kannon]]. They were not originally made of paper, they were first made from wooden slats that were hung from the gates of Kannon temples by nails made of bamboo. The slats were carved out with the visitors' name, area of origin and often included a prayer for a good life and afterlife.

The [[inkstick|ink]] used is so strong that after the printed {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} are placed on the shrine or temple gate, years later when the paper is peeled away, the ink remains. Therefore, many shrine {{transl|ja|[[kannushi]]}} or {{transl|ja|[[shinshoku]]}} do not like the use of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}}, as well as more modern practices, where younger {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} practitioners do not pray or buy a stamp from the shrine before applying their {{transl|ja|senjafuda}}.
[[File:Hiroshige senjafuda.jpg|thumb|upright=1.3|[[Hiroshige]] {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} depicting an exchange meeting in 1842. During the beginning of the Edo period, shrine pilgrimages gained popularity, beginning the tradition known as {{transl|ja|[[senjamairi]]}}, meaning "a thousand shrine visits for good luck". {{transl|ja|Kokan nosatsu senjafuda}} are a lot more colorful than {{transl|ja|saimei nosatsu}}, and have rich patterns and designs, being used more as novelty items and more like trading cards or the business cards of today. Like many things during the Edo period, {{transl|ja|kokan nosatsu senjafuda}} were regulated, with the number of colours on a person's {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} limited to their class and place in society.
Because of this, collectors who enjoyed the many designs and colors of {{transl|ja|senjafuda}} began meeting to exchange them with one another; first, the meetings took place at private homes, and then later were arranged for public places like restaurants and expensive [[ochaya|tea houses]].
