[[en:Senjafuda]]
<!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Senjafuda}} [[fr:Senjafuda]] [[ja:千社札]] [[pl:Senja-fuda]] [[zh:千社札]] {{wikidata link|Q335706}} [[Category:Buddhism in the Edo period]] [[Category:Buddhist religious objects]] [[Category:Culture of Japan]] [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shinto religious objects]] [[Category:Ukiyo-e]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Senjafuda]][[en:Senjafuda]][[fr:Senjafuda]][[ja:千社札]][[pl:Senja-fuda]][[zh:千社札]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Senjafuda]][[en:Senjafuda]][[fr:Senjafuda]][[ja:千社札]][[pl:Senja-fuda]][[zh:千社札]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Senjafuda]][[en:Senjafuda]][[fr:Senjafuda]][[ja:千社札]][[pl:Senja-fuda]][[zh:千社札]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q335706]] Wikidata name: Senjafuda [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Handwritten or woodblock-printed pasted to shrine or temple in Japan}} {{Refimprove|date=February 2018}} {{Italic title}}{{Shinto Talismans|image=[[File:八坂神社 (4472701259).jpg|thumb|Examples on a ceiling at [[Yasaka Shrine]]]]}} [[File:Gifu-kegonji0059.JPG|right|thumb|300px|{{transliteration|ja|Senjafuda}} pasted on a shrine gate in [[:en:Gifu|Gifu]]]]
{{nihongo3|{{lit|thousand-shrine tags}}||'''Senjafuda'''}} are [[:en:Votive|Votive]] slips, [[:en:stickers|stickers]] or [[:en:Placard|Placard]]s [[:en:Poster|posted]] on the gates or buildings of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s and [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] in [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Unlike {{transliteration|ja|[[ofuda]]}}, which bear the name of the shrine, {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} bear the name of the worshipper, and can be purchased pre-printed with common names at temples and shrines throughout Japan, as well as at stationery stores and video game centres. {{transliteration|ja|Senjafuda}} were originally made from wooden slats, but have been made of paper since the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].
A single senjafuda measures 1.6 {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Cun (unit)|sun]]}} ({{cvt|58|mm}}) in width and 4.8 {{transl|ja|sun}} ({{cvt|173|mm}}) in height. This gives the {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} a ratio of 1:3. A frame is drawn inside this space which contains the lettering or pictures. In 1887, a measurement for this frame was also established as {{cvt|48|mm}} wide and {{cvt|144|mm}} tall.
Ordinarily, the designs were used to commemorate a visit to a temple or shrine and printed with simple monochromatic schemes, but eventually aesthetic sense gave way to colorful variations and designs. In the pleasure quarters of [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], colorful designs were employed on {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} and used in place of traditional business cards. This variation is called {{transliteration|ja|hana-meishi}} which roughly translated to "flower business card." Today, the "business card" use of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} is the most common.
{{transliteration|ja|Senjafuda}} were primarily printed with {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Edomoji|Edomoji]]}}, or Edo-period lettering styles, and pressed with the same traditional wooden boards used to produce [[ukiyo-e]] prints. Stickers on shrines are often pasted in very obvious, easily seen locations, but a variation on this practice is to purposely obscure the location of the {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} in order to protect it from exposure to wind and rain and thus prolong its presence.
== History == {{transliteration|ja|Senjafuda}} were first produced in the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] (794–1185) when shrine worshipers made pilgrimages to visits to many shrines and worship the Buddhist goddess of mercy, [[Kannon]]. They were not originally made of paper, they were first made from wooden slats that were hung from the gates of Kannon temples by nails made of [[:en:Bamboo|Bamboo]]. The slats were carved out with the visitors' name, area of origin and often included a prayer for a good life and [[:en:Afterlife|Afterlife]].
There are two styles of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}}: the older style, {{transliteration|ja|daimei nosatsu}}, and the newer style, {{transliteration|ja|kokan nosatsu}}. {{transliteration|ja|Daimei nosatsu}} are basic black ink on white paper. The [[:en:Inkstick|ink]] used is so strong that after the printed {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} are placed on the [[Torii|shrine or temple gate]], years later when the paper is peeled away, the ink remains. Therefore, many shrine {{transliteration|ja|[[kannushi]]}} or {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Shinshoku|Shinshoku]]}} do not like the use of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}}, as well as more modern practices, where younger {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} practitioners do not pray or buy a stamp from the shrine before applying their {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}}.
[[File:Hiroshige senjafuda.jpg|thumb|upright=1.3|[[Hiroshige]] {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} depicting an exchange meeting in 1842. One of the hanging banners ironically states "Graffiti strictly forbidden".]] The later style of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} are called {{transliteration|ja|kokan nosatsu}} and originated in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] (1603–1868). During the beginning of the Edo period, shrine pilgrimages gained popularity, beginning the tradition known as {{transliteration|ja|[[senjamairi]]}}, meaning "a thousand shrine visits for good luck". {{transliteration|ja|Kokan nosatsu senjafuda}} are a lot more colorful than {{transliteration|ja|saimei nosatsu}}, and have rich patterns and designs, being used more as novelty items and more like trading cards or the business cards of today. Like many things during the Edo period, {{transliteration|ja|kokan nosatsu senjafuda}} were regulated, with the number of colours on a person's {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} limited to their class and place in society.
Because of this, collectors who enjoyed the many designs and colors of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} began meeting to exchange them with one another;<ref name="UofOregon">{{cite web | title=The world of senjafuda | website=Mellon Projects | url=https://glam.uoregon.edu/s/yokai-senjafuda/page/world-of-senjafuda | access-date=24 November 2022}}</ref> first, the meetings took place at private homes, and then later were arranged for public places like restaurants and expensive [[:en:Ochaya|tea houses]]. According to Kiritani's ''Vanishing Japan'', the oldest surviving invitation card to a {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} meeting dates back to 1799.<ref>Kiritani, Elizabeth ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=5_GGbiw1-y4C&dq=vanishing+japan&pg=PA1 Vanishing Japan]''</ref> Due to the growing popularity of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} meetings, the [[Government of Japan|Japanese government]] enforced a law forbidding their trading, which did not stop the meetings from taking place. {{transliteration|ja|Senjafuda}} meetings continue to this day, with collectors and aficionados alike meeting to share and trade their own designs as well as admire others.
US collector and Japanese anthropologist [[:en:Frederick Starr|Frederick Starr]] was a turn-of-the-century collector and avid participant in {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} or {{transliteration|ja|nōsatsu-kai}} (votive slip exchange clubs), so much so that he was given the name "Dr. Ofuda". He collected tens of thousands of slips, and a fellow collector and popular art enthusiast, [[:en:Gertrude Bass Warner|Gertrude Bass Warner]], purchased much of his collection. It currently resides at the [[:en:University of Oregon|University of Oregon]] Knight Library Special Collections & University Archives, part of the Gertrude Bass Warner Collection, and examples are viewable online at UO Oregon Digital.<ref>{{cite web|last1=McDowell|first1=Kevin|title=Gertrude Bass Warner Collection of Japanese Shrine and Temple Votive Slips (nōsatsu)|url=https://oregondigital.org/sets/gb-warner-nosatsu|website=Oregon Digital|publisher=University of Oregon|access-date=3 May 2017}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|last1=McDowell|first1=Kevin|title=Rare Collection: Nōsatsu Japanese Shrine and Temple Votive Slips|url=https://blogs.uoregon.edu/scua/2016/05/09/rare-collection-nosatsu-japanese-shrine-and-temple-votive-slips/|website=Upbound UO Blog|publisher=Special Collections & University Archives, University of Oregon|access-date=3 May 2017}}</ref>

The pilgrims used to carry walking staffs for their long journeys, which doubled as an applicator for {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}}. The paste was applied with something called {{transliteration|ja|meotobake}} – two brushes about 30 degrees apart, with a clip on the other side of the brushes, allowing {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} to be pasted in out of reach areas, leaving others to wonder exactly how they got up there.
In the present day, {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} are made from printed paper, and are rarely made traditionally through wood block printing. Wooden slat {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}}, however, are still produced, and are worn as a [[:en:Necklace|Necklace]] or used for [[:en:keychain|key chain]] and [[:en:Mobile phone accessories|cell phone ornaments]]. The ones made from paper are pre-printed with common names; machines are also available that can produce custom {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} with adhesive backings.

The pilgrims used to carry walking staffs for their long journeys, which doubled as an applicator for {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}}. The paste was applied with something called {{transliteration|ja|meotobake}} – two brushes about 30 degrees apart, with a clip on the other side of the brushes, allowing {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} to be pasted in out of reach areas, leaving others to wonder exactly how they got up there.
In the present day, {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} are made from printed paper, and are rarely made traditionally through wood block printing. Wooden slat {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}}, however, are still produced, and are worn as a [[:en:Necklace|Necklace]] or used for [[:en:keychain|key chain]] and [[:en:Mobile phone accessories|cell phone ornaments]]. The ones made from paper are pre-printed with common names; machines are also available that can produce custom {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} with adhesive backings.
== Famous figures == Some famous producers of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} are Hiroshige, Eisen, Kunisada, Kuniyoshi. They mainly produced {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}}, due to the expensive of the ukiyo-e printing process.
Senrei Sekioka was one of the foremost Japanese experts of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} history; Iseman and Frederick Starr were also important members of the {{transliteration|ja|nosatsu-kai}} during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji]] and [[:en:Taishō era|Taishō]] eras.
== Modern-day {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} == {{transliteration|ja|Senjafuda}} are also sold as [[:en:Sticker|stickers]] which do not require separate paste. As stickers, they are also placed in [[:en:Book|books]] and on personal items for identification and decoration. A common criticism of the sticker version of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} is that they are more difficult to peel off than their original pasted ancestors, and thus can disfigure the underlying buildings when removed.
==Gallery== <gallery mode="packed" heights="230"> File:Yokai senjafuda.jpg|[[Nōsatsu]], Shōki the Demon Queller File:Senjafuda collector.jpg|Masked {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} collector tossing slips into the air File:Senjafuda foxes.jpg|Fox hairdressers File:Senjafuda fox at gates.jpg|A stone fox statue in front of a series of shrine gates at [[Anamori Inari]], Tokyo File:Senjafuda paster.jpg|Tengu Kōhei with a box for his {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} and pole-mounted brushes used for pasting </gallery>
==See also== * {{transliteration|ja|[[Ofuda]]}}
==Notes== {{Reflist}}
==References== * Gordenker, Alice, "[http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/ek20101118wh.html So, What the Heck is That? Shrine tags]", ''[[:en:Japan Times|Japan Times]]'', 18 November 2010, p. 13.
== External links == * {{Commons category-inline}} * [https://glam.uoregon.edu/yokaisenjafuda/page/world-of-senjafuda World of Senjafuda] University of Oregon * [https://www.miniaturejapanesewoodblocks.net/gallery-6-senjafuda-votive-slips Gallery of senjafuda] Christenson Collection of Miniature Japanese Woodblock Prints
{{ukiyo-e}} <!--this was an inappropriate and erroneous transclusion meant to be a bottom bar thing I think but not including--> {{Amulets and Talismans}} {{Authority control}}
[[wiktionary:千社札|千社札]]
[[en:Senjafuda]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Senjafuda}} [[fr:Senjafuda]] [[ja:千社札]] [[pl:Senja-fuda]] [[zh:千社札]] {{wikidata link|Q335706}} [[Category:Buddhism in the Edo period]] [[Category:Buddhist religious objects]] [[Category:Culture of Japan]] [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shinto religious objects]] [[Category:Ukiyo-e]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Senjafuda]][[en:Senjafuda]][[fr:Senjafuda]][[ja:千社札]][[pl:Senja-fuda]][[zh:千社札]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Senjafuda]][[en:Senjafuda]][[fr:Senjafuda]][[ja:千社札]][[pl:Senja-fuda]][[zh:千社札]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Senjafuda]][[en:Senjafuda]][[fr:Senjafuda]][[ja:千社札]][[pl:Senja-fuda]][[zh:千社札]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q335706]] Wikidata name: Senjafuda [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Handwritten or woodblock-printed pasted to shrine or temple in Japan}} {{Refimprove|date=February 2018}} {{Italic title}}{{Shinto Talismans|image=[[File:八坂神社 (4472701259).jpg|thumb|Examples on a ceiling at [[Yasaka Shrine]]]]}} [[File:Gifu-kegonji0059.JPG|right|thumb|300px|{{transliteration|ja|Senjafuda}} pasted on a shrine gate in [[:en:Gifu|Gifu]]]]
{{nihongo3|{{lit|thousand-shrine tags}}||'''Senjafuda'''}} are [[:en:Votive|Votive]] slips, [[:en:stickers|stickers]] or [[:en:Placard|Placard]]s [[:en:Poster|posted]] on the gates or buildings of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s and [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] in [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Unlike {{transliteration|ja|[[ofuda]]}}, which bear the name of the shrine, {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} bear the name of the worshipper, and can be purchased pre-printed with common names at temples and shrines throughout Japan, as well as at stationery stores and video game centres. {{transliteration|ja|Senjafuda}} were originally made from wooden slats, but have been made of paper since the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].
A single senjafuda measures 1.6 {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Cun (unit)|sun]]}} ({{cvt|58|mm}}) in width and 4.8 {{transl|ja|sun}} ({{cvt|173|mm}}) in height. This gives the {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} a ratio of 1:3. A frame is drawn inside this space which contains the lettering or pictures. In 1887, a measurement for this frame was also established as {{cvt|48|mm}} wide and {{cvt|144|mm}} tall.
Ordinarily, the designs were used to commemorate a visit to a temple or shrine and printed with simple monochromatic schemes, but eventually aesthetic sense gave way to colorful variations and designs. In the pleasure quarters of [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], colorful designs were employed on {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} and used in place of traditional business cards. This variation is called {{transliteration|ja|hana-meishi}} which roughly translated to "flower business card." Today, the "business card" use of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} is the most common.
{{transliteration|ja|Senjafuda}} were primarily printed with {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Edomoji|Edomoji]]}}, or Edo-period lettering styles, and pressed with the same traditional wooden boards used to produce [[ukiyo-e]] prints. Stickers on shrines are often pasted in very obvious, easily seen locations, but a variation on this practice is to purposely obscure the location of the {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} in order to protect it from exposure to wind and rain and thus prolong its presence.
== History == {{transliteration|ja|Senjafuda}} were first produced in the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] (794–1185) when shrine worshipers made pilgrimages to visits to many shrines and worship the Buddhist goddess of mercy, [[Kannon]]. They were not originally made of paper, they were first made from wooden slats that were hung from the gates of Kannon temples by nails made of [[:en:Bamboo|Bamboo]]. The slats were carved out with the visitors' name, area of origin and often included a prayer for a good life and [[:en:Afterlife|Afterlife]].
There are two styles of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}}: the older style, {{transliteration|ja|daimei nosatsu}}, and the newer style, {{transliteration|ja|kokan nosatsu}}. {{transliteration|ja|Daimei nosatsu}} are basic black ink on white paper. The [[:en:Inkstick|ink]] used is so strong that after the printed {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} are placed on the [[Torii|shrine or temple gate]], years later when the paper is peeled away, the ink remains. Therefore, many shrine {{transliteration|ja|[[kannushi]]}} or {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Shinshoku|Shinshoku]]}} do not like the use of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}}, as well as more modern practices, where younger {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} practitioners do not pray or buy a stamp from the shrine before applying their {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}}.
[[File:Hiroshige senjafuda.jpg|thumb|upright=1.3|[[Hiroshige]] {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} depicting an exchange meeting in 1842. One of the hanging banners ironically states "Graffiti strictly forbidden".]] The later style of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} are called {{transliteration|ja|kokan nosatsu}} and originated in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] (1603–1868). During the beginning of the Edo period, shrine pilgrimages gained popularity, beginning the tradition known as {{transliteration|ja|[[senjamairi]]}}, meaning "a thousand shrine visits for good luck". {{transliteration|ja|Kokan nosatsu senjafuda}} are a lot more colorful than {{transliteration|ja|saimei nosatsu}}, and have rich patterns and designs, being used more as novelty items and more like trading cards or the business cards of today. Like many things during the Edo period, {{transliteration|ja|kokan nosatsu senjafuda}} were regulated, with the number of colours on a person's {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} limited to their class and place in society.
Because of this, collectors who enjoyed the many designs and colors of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} began meeting to exchange them with one another;<ref name="UofOregon">{{cite web | title=The world of senjafuda | website=Mellon Projects | url=https://glam.uoregon.edu/s/yokai-senjafuda/page/world-of-senjafuda | access-date=24 November 2022}}</ref> first, the meetings took place at private homes, and then later were arranged for public places like restaurants and expensive [[:en:Ochaya|tea houses]]. According to Kiritani's ''Vanishing Japan'', the oldest surviving invitation card to a {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} meeting dates back to 1799.<ref>Kiritani, Elizabeth ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=5_GGbiw1-y4C&dq=vanishing+japan&pg=PA1 Vanishing Japan]''</ref> Due to the growing popularity of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} meetings, the [[Government of Japan|Japanese government]] enforced a law forbidding their trading, which did not stop the meetings from taking place. {{transliteration|ja|Senjafuda}} meetings continue to this day, with collectors and aficionados alike meeting to share and trade their own designs as well as admire others.
US collector and Japanese anthropologist [[:en:Frederick Starr|Frederick Starr]] was a turn-of-the-century collector and avid participant in {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} or {{transliteration|ja|nōsatsu-kai}} (votive slip exchange clubs), so much so that he was given the name "Dr. Ofuda". He collected tens of thousands of slips, and a fellow collector and popular art enthusiast, [[:en:Gertrude Bass Warner|Gertrude Bass Warner]], purchased much of his collection. It currently resides at the [[:en:University of Oregon|University of Oregon]] Knight Library Special Collections & University Archives, part of the Gertrude Bass Warner Collection, and examples are viewable online at UO Oregon Digital.<ref>{{cite web|last1=McDowell|first1=Kevin|title=Gertrude Bass Warner Collection of Japanese Shrine and Temple Votive Slips (nōsatsu)|url=https://oregondigital.org/sets/gb-warner-nosatsu|website=Oregon Digital|publisher=University of Oregon|access-date=3 May 2017}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|last1=McDowell|first1=Kevin|title=Rare Collection: Nōsatsu Japanese Shrine and Temple Votive Slips|url=https://blogs.uoregon.edu/scua/2016/05/09/rare-collection-nosatsu-japanese-shrine-and-temple-votive-slips/|website=Upbound UO Blog|publisher=Special Collections & University Archives, University of Oregon|access-date=3 May 2017}}</ref>
== Construction == {{transliteration|ja|Senjafuda}} used to be made from [[:en:Rice paper|Rice paper]] with ink called {{transliteration|ja|sumi}}, and were pasted on with a starchy rice paste. The pilgrims used to carry walking staffs for their long journeys, which doubled as an applicator for {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}}. The paste was applied with something called {{transliteration|ja|meotobake}} – two brushes about 30 degrees apart, with a clip on the other side of the brushes, allowing {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} to be pasted in out of reach areas, leaving others to wonder exactly how they got up there.
In the present day, {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} are made from printed paper, and are rarely made traditionally through wood block printing. Wooden slat {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}}, however, are still produced, and are worn as a [[:en:Necklace|Necklace]] or used for [[:en:keychain|key chain]] and [[:en:Mobile phone accessories|cell phone ornaments]]. The ones made from paper are pre-printed with common names; machines are also available that can produce custom {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} with adhesive backings.
== Famous figures == Some famous producers of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} are Hiroshige, Eisen, Kunisada, Kuniyoshi. They mainly produced {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}}, due to the expensive of the ukiyo-e printing process.
Senrei Sekioka was one of the foremost Japanese experts of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} history; Iseman and Frederick Starr were also important members of the {{transliteration|ja|nosatsu-kai}} during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji]] and [[:en:Taishō era|Taishō]] eras.
== Modern-day {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} == {{transliteration|ja|Senjafuda}} are also sold as [[:en:Sticker|stickers]] which do not require separate paste. As stickers, they are also placed in [[:en:Book|books]] and on personal items for identification and decoration. A common criticism of the sticker version of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} is that they are more difficult to peel off than their original pasted ancestors, and thus can disfigure the underlying buildings when removed.
==Gallery== <gallery mode="packed" heights="230"> File:Yokai senjafuda.jpg|[[Nōsatsu]], Shōki the Demon Queller File:Senjafuda collector.jpg|Masked {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} collector tossing slips into the air File:Senjafuda foxes.jpg|Fox hairdressers File:Senjafuda fox at gates.jpg|A stone fox statue in front of a series of shrine gates at [[Anamori Inari]], Tokyo File:Senjafuda paster.jpg|Tengu Kōhei with a box for his {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} and pole-mounted brushes used for pasting </gallery>

In 1887, a measurement for this frame was also established as {{cvt|48|mm}} wide and {{cvt|144|mm}} tall.
Ordinarily, the designs were used to commemorate a visit to a temple or shrine and printed with simple monochromatic schemes, but eventually aesthetic sense gave way to colorful variations and designs. In the pleasure quarters of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}, colorful designs were employed on {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} and used in place of traditional business cards. This variation is called {{transliteration|ja|hana-meishi}} which roughly translated to "flower business card." Today, the "business card" use of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} is the most common.
{{transliteration|ja|Senjafuda}} were primarily printed with {{transliteration|ja|{{ill|Edomoji|en|Edomoji|WD=Q1133518|lt=Edomoji}}}}, or Edo-period lettering styles, and pressed with the same traditional wooden boards used to produce [[ukiyo-e]] prints. Stickers on shrines are often pasted in very obvious, easily seen locations, but a variation on this practice is to purposely obscure the location of the {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} in order to protect it from exposure to wind and rain and thus prolong its presence.

They were not originally made of paper, they were first made from wooden slats that were hung from the gates of Kannon temples by nails made of {{ill|Bamboo|en|Bamboo|WD=Q670887|lt=Bamboo}}. The slats were carved out with the visitors' name, area of origin and often included a prayer for a good life and {{ill|Afterlife|en|Afterlife|WD=Q623282|lt=Afterlife}}.
There are two styles of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}}: the older style, {{transliteration|ja|daimei nosatsu}}, and the newer style, {{transliteration|ja|kokan nosatsu}}. {{transliteration|ja|Daimei nosatsu}} are basic black ink on white paper. The {{ill|Inkstick|en|Inkstick|WD=Q11240146|lt=ink}} used is so strong that after the printed {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} are placed on the [[Torii|shrine or temple gate]], years later when the paper is peeled away, the ink remains. Therefore, many shrine {{transliteration|ja|[[kannushi]]}} or {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Shinshoku|Shinshoku]]}} do not like the use of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}}, as well as more modern practices, where younger {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} practitioners do not pray or buy a stamp from the shrine before applying their {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}}.
[[File:Hiroshige senjafuda.jpg|thumb|upright=1.3|[[Hiroshige]] {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} depicting an exchange meeting in 1842. One of the hanging banners ironically states "Graffiti strictly forbidden".]] The later style of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} are called {{transliteration|ja|kokan nosatsu}} and originated in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} (1603–1868). During the beginning of the Edo period, shrine pilgrimages gained popularity, beginning the tradition known as {{transliteration|ja|[[senjamairi]]}}, meaning "a thousand shrine visits for good luck". {{transliteration|ja|Kokan nosatsu senjafuda}} are a lot more colorful than {{transliteration|ja|saimei nosatsu}}, and have rich patterns and designs, being used more as novelty items and more like trading cards or the business cards of today. Because of this, collectors who enjoyed the many designs and colors of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} began meeting to exchange them with one another;<ref name="UofOregon">{{cite web | title=The world of senjafuda | website=Mellon Projects | url=https://glam.uoregon.edu/s/yokai-senjafuda/page/world-of-senjafuda | access-date=24 November 2022}}</ref> first, the meetings took place at private homes, and then later were arranged for public places like restaurants and expensive {{ill|Ochaya|en|Ochaya|WD=Q7076300|lt=tea houses}}. According to Kiritani's ''Vanishing Japan'', the oldest surviving invitation card to a {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} meeting dates back to 1799.<ref>Kiritani, Elizabeth ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=5_GGbiw1-y4C&dq=vanishing+japan&pg=PA1 Vanishing Japan]''</ref> Due to the growing popularity of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} meetings, the [[Government of Japan|Japanese government]] enforced a law forbidding their trading, which did not stop the meetings from taking place. {{transliteration|ja|Senjafuda}} meetings continue to this day, with collectors and aficionados alike meeting to share and trade their own designs as well as admire others.
US collector and Japanese anthropologist {{ill|Frederick Starr|en|Frederick Starr|WD=Q5498791|lt=Frederick Starr}} was a turn-of-the-century collector and avid participant in {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} or {{transliteration|ja|nōsatsu-kai}} (votive slip exchange clubs), so much so that he was given the name "Dr. Ofuda". He collected tens of thousands of slips, and a fellow collector and popular art enthusiast, {{ill|Gertrude Bass Warner|en|Gertrude Bass Warner|WD=Q54867731|lt=Gertrude Bass Warner}}, purchased much of his collection. It currently resides at the {{ill|University of Oregon|en|University of Oregon|WD=Q766145|lt=University of Oregon}} Knight Library Special Collections & University Archives, part of the Gertrude Bass Warner Collection, and examples are viewable online at UO Oregon Digital.<ref>{{cite web|last1=McDowell|first1=Kevin|title=Gertrude Bass Warner Collection of Japanese Shrine and Temple Votive Slips (nōsatsu)|url=https://oregondigital.org/sets/gb-warner-nosatsu|website=Oregon Digital|publisher=University of Oregon|access-date=3 May 2017}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|last1=McDowell|first1=Kevin|title=Rare Collection: Nōsatsu Japanese Shrine and Temple Votive Slips|url=https://blogs.uoregon.edu/scua/2016/05/09/rare-collection-nosatsu-japanese-shrine-and-temple-votive-slips/|website=Upbound UO Blog|publisher=Special Collections & University Archives, University of Oregon|access-date=3 May 2017}}</ref>
== Construction == {{transliteration|ja|Senjafuda}} used to be made from {{ill|Rice paper|en|Rice paper|WD=Q1066536|lt=Rice paper}} with ink called {{transliteration|ja|sumi}}, and were pasted on with a starchy rice paste. The pilgrims used to carry walking staffs for their long journeys, which doubled as an applicator for {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}}. The paste was applied with something called {{transliteration|ja|meotobake}} – two brushes about 30 degrees apart, with a clip on the other side of the brushes, allowing {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} to be pasted in out of reach areas, leaving others to wonder exactly how they got up there.
In the present day, {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} are made from printed paper, and are rarely made traditionally through wood block printing. Wooden slat {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}}, however, are still produced, and are worn as a {{ill|Necklace|en|Necklace|WD=Q189299|lt=Necklace}} or used for {{ill|keychain|en|keychain|WD=Q51338|lt=key chain}} and {{ill|Mobile phone accessories|en|Mobile phone accessories|WD=Q6887188|lt=cell phone ornaments}}. The ones made from paper are pre-printed with common names; machines are also available that can produce custom {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} with adhesive backings.
== Famous figures == Some famous producers of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} are Hiroshige, Eisen, Kunisada, Kuniyoshi. They mainly produced {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}}, due to the expensive of the ukiyo-e printing process.
Senrei Sekioka was one of the foremost Japanese experts of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} history; Iseman and Frederick Starr were also important members of the {{transliteration|ja|nosatsu-kai}} during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji}} and {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō}} eras.
== Modern-day {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} == {{transliteration|ja|Senjafuda}} are also sold as {{ill|Sticker|en|Sticker|WD=Q2872553|lt=stickers}} which do not require separate paste. As stickers, they are also placed in {{ill|Book|en|Book|WD=Q571|lt=books}} and on personal items for identification and decoration. A common criticism of the sticker version of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} is that they are more difficult to peel off than their original pasted ancestors, and thus can disfigure the underlying buildings when removed.
==Gallery== <gallery mode="packed" heights="230"> File:Yokai senjafuda.jpg|[[Nōsatsu]], Shōki the Demon Queller File:Senjafuda collector.jpg|Masked {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} collector tossing slips into the air File:Senjafuda foxes.jpg|Fox hairdressers File:Senjafuda fox at gates.jpg|A stone fox statue in front of a series of shrine gates at [[Anamori Inari]], Tokyo File:Senjafuda paster.jpg|Tengu Kōhei with a box for his {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} and pole-mounted brushes used for pasting </gallery>
==See also== * {{transliteration|ja|[[Ofuda]]}}
==Notes== {{Reflist}}
==References== * Gordenker, Alice, "[http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/ek20101118wh.html So, What the Heck is That? Shrine tags]", ''[[:en:Japan Times|Japan Times]]'', 18 November 2010, p. 13.

[[wiktionary:千社札|千社札]]
[[en:Senjafuda]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Senjafuda}} [[fr:Senjafuda]] [[ja:千社札]] [[pl:Senja-fuda]] [[zh:千社札]] {{wikidata link|Q335706}} [[Category:Buddhism in the Edo period]] [[Category:Buddhist religious objects]] [[Category:Culture of Japan]] [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shinto religious objects]] [[Category:Ukiyo-e]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Senjafuda]][[en:Senjafuda]][[fr:Senjafuda]][[ja:千社札]][[pl:Senja-fuda]][[zh:千社札]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Senjafuda]][[en:Senjafuda]][[fr:Senjafuda]][[ja:千社札]][[pl:Senja-fuda]][[zh:千社札]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Senjafuda]][[en:Senjafuda]][[fr:Senjafuda]][[ja:千社札]][[pl:Senja-fuda]][[zh:千社札]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q335706]] Wikidata name: Senjafuda [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Handwritten or woodblock-printed pasted to shrine or temple in Japan}} {{Refimprove|date=February 2018}} {{Italic title}}{{Shinto Talismans|image=[[File:八坂神社 (4472701259).jpg|thumb|Examples on a ceiling at [[Yasaka Shrine]]]]}} [[File:Gifu-kegonji0059.JPG|right|thumb|300px|{{transliteration|ja|Senjafuda}} pasted on a shrine gate in {{ill|Gifu|en|Gifu|WD=Q45798|lt=Gifu}}]]
{{nihongo3|{{lit|thousand-shrine tags}}||'''Senjafuda'''}} are [[:en:Votive|Votive]] slips, [[:en:stickers|stickers]] or {{ill|Placard|en|Placard|WD=Q606876|lt=Placard}}s {{ill|Poster|en|Poster|WD=Q429785|lt=posted}} on the gates or buildings of {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}}s and {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|WD=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temples}} in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. Unlike {{transliteration|ja|[[ofuda]]}}, which bear the name of the shrine, {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} bear the name of the worshipper, and can be purchased pre-printed with common names at temples and shrines throughout Japan, as well as at stationery stores and video game centres. {{transliteration|ja|Senjafuda}} were originally made from wooden slats, but have been made of paper since the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}.
A single senjafuda measures 1.6 {{transliteration|ja|{{ill|Cun (unit)|en|Cun (unit)|WD=Q876237|lt=sun}}}} ({{cvt|58|mm}}) in width and 4.8 {{transl|ja|sun}} ({{cvt|173|mm}}) in height. This gives the {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} a ratio of 1:3. A frame is drawn inside this space which contains the lettering or pictures. In 1887, a measurement for this frame was also established as {{cvt|48|mm}} wide and {{cvt|144|mm}} tall.
Ordinarily, the designs were used to commemorate a visit to a temple or shrine and printed with simple monochromatic schemes, but eventually aesthetic sense gave way to colorful variations and designs. In the pleasure quarters of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}, colorful designs were employed on {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} and used in place of traditional business cards. This variation is called {{transliteration|ja|hana-meishi}} which roughly translated to "flower business card." Today, the "business card" use of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} is the most common.
{{transliteration|ja|Senjafuda}} were primarily printed with {{transliteration|ja|{{ill|Edomoji|en|Edomoji|WD=Q1133518|lt=Edomoji}}}}, or Edo-period lettering styles, and pressed with the same traditional wooden boards used to produce [[ukiyo-e]] prints. Stickers on shrines are often pasted in very obvious, easily seen locations, but a variation on this practice is to purposely obscure the location of the {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} in order to protect it from exposure to wind and rain and thus prolong its presence.
== History == {{transliteration|ja|Senjafuda}} were first produced in the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}} (794–1185) when shrine worshipers made pilgrimages to visits to many shrines and worship the Buddhist goddess of mercy, [[Kannon]]. They were not originally made of paper, they were first made from wooden slats that were hung from the gates of Kannon temples by nails made of {{ill|Bamboo|en|Bamboo|WD=Q670887|lt=Bamboo}}. The slats were carved out with the visitors' name, area of origin and often included a prayer for a good life and {{ill|Afterlife|en|Afterlife|WD=Q623282|lt=Afterlife}}.
There are two styles of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}}: the older style, {{transliteration|ja|daimei nosatsu}}, and the newer style, {{transliteration|ja|kokan nosatsu}}. {{transliteration|ja|Daimei nosatsu}} are basic black ink on white paper. The {{ill|Inkstick|en|Inkstick|WD=Q11240146|lt=ink}} used is so strong that after the printed {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} are placed on the [[Torii|shrine or temple gate]], years later when the paper is peeled away, the ink remains. Therefore, many shrine {{transliteration|ja|[[kannushi]]}} or {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Shinshoku|Shinshoku]]}} do not like the use of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}}, as well as more modern practices, where younger {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} practitioners do not pray or buy a stamp from the shrine before applying their {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}}.
[[File:Hiroshige senjafuda.jpg|thumb|upright=1.3|[[Hiroshige]] {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} depicting an exchange meeting in 1842. One of the hanging banners ironically states "Graffiti strictly forbidden".]] The later style of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} are called {{transliteration|ja|kokan nosatsu}} and originated in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} (1603–1868). During the beginning of the Edo period, shrine pilgrimages gained popularity, beginning the tradition known as {{transliteration|ja|[[senjamairi]]}}, meaning "a thousand shrine visits for good luck". {{transliteration|ja|Kokan nosatsu senjafuda}} are a lot more colorful than {{transliteration|ja|saimei nosatsu}}, and have rich patterns and designs, being used more as novelty items and more like trading cards or the business cards of today. Like many things during the Edo period, {{transliteration|ja|kokan nosatsu senjafuda}} were regulated, with the number of colours on a person's {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} limited to their class and place in society.
Because of this, collectors who enjoyed the many designs and colors of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} began meeting to exchange them with one another;<ref name="UofOregon">{{cite web | title=The world of senjafuda | website=Mellon Projects | url=https://glam.uoregon.edu/s/yokai-senjafuda/page/world-of-senjafuda | access-date=24 November 2022}}</ref> first, the meetings took place at private homes, and then later were arranged for public places like restaurants and expensive {{ill|Ochaya|en|Ochaya|WD=Q7076300|lt=tea houses}}. According to Kiritani's ''Vanishing Japan'', the oldest surviving invitation card to a {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} meeting dates back to 1799.<ref>Kiritani, Elizabeth ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=5_GGbiw1-y4C&dq=vanishing+japan&pg=PA1 Vanishing Japan]''</ref> Due to the growing popularity of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} meetings, the [[Government of Japan|Japanese government]] enforced a law forbidding their trading, which did not stop the meetings from taking place. {{transliteration|ja|Senjafuda}} meetings continue to this day, with collectors and aficionados alike meeting to share and trade their own designs as well as admire others.
US collector and Japanese anthropologist {{ill|Frederick Starr|en|Frederick Starr|WD=Q5498791|lt=Frederick Starr}} was a turn-of-the-century collector and avid participant in {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} or {{transliteration|ja|nōsatsu-kai}} (votive slip exchange clubs), so much so that he was given the name "Dr. Ofuda". He collected tens of thousands of slips, and a fellow collector and popular art enthusiast, {{ill|Gertrude Bass Warner|en|Gertrude Bass Warner|WD=Q54867731|lt=Gertrude Bass Warner}}, purchased much of his collection. It currently resides at the {{ill|University of Oregon|en|University of Oregon|WD=Q766145|lt=University of Oregon}} Knight Library Special Collections & University Archives, part of the Gertrude Bass Warner Collection, and examples are viewable online at UO Oregon Digital.<ref>{{cite web|last1=McDowell|first1=Kevin|title=Gertrude Bass Warner Collection of Japanese Shrine and Temple Votive Slips (nōsatsu)|url=https://oregondigital.org/sets/gb-warner-nosatsu|website=Oregon Digital|publisher=University of Oregon|access-date=3 May 2017}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|last1=McDowell|first1=Kevin|title=Rare Collection: Nōsatsu Japanese Shrine and Temple Votive Slips|url=https://blogs.uoregon.edu/scua/2016/05/09/rare-collection-nosatsu-japanese-shrine-and-temple-votive-slips/|website=Upbound UO Blog|publisher=Special Collections & University Archives, University of Oregon|access-date=3 May 2017}}</ref>
== Construction == {{transliteration|ja|Senjafuda}} used to be made from {{ill|Rice paper|en|Rice paper|WD=Q1066536|lt=Rice paper}} with ink called {{transliteration|ja|sumi}}, and were pasted on with a starchy rice paste. The pilgrims used to carry walking staffs for their long journeys, which doubled as an applicator for {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}}. The paste was applied with something called {{transliteration|ja|meotobake}} – two brushes about 30 degrees apart, with a clip on the other side of the brushes, allowing {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} to be pasted in out of reach areas, leaving others to wonder exactly how they got up there.
In the present day, {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} are made from printed paper, and are rarely made traditionally through wood block printing. Wooden slat {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}}, however, are still produced, and are worn as a {{ill|Necklace|en|Necklace|WD=Q189299|lt=Necklace}} or used for {{ill|keychain|en|keychain|WD=Q51338|lt=key chain}} and {{ill|Mobile phone accessories|en|Mobile phone accessories|WD=Q6887188|lt=cell phone ornaments}}. The ones made from paper are pre-printed with common names; machines are also available that can produce custom {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} with adhesive backings.
== Famous figures == Some famous producers of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} are Hiroshige, Eisen, Kunisada, Kuniyoshi. They mainly produced {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}}, due to the expensive of the ukiyo-e printing process.
Senrei Sekioka was one of the foremost Japanese experts of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} history; Iseman and Frederick Starr were also important members of the {{transliteration|ja|nosatsu-kai}} during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji}} and {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō}} eras.
== Modern-day {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} == {{transliteration|ja|Senjafuda}} are also sold as {{ill|Sticker|en|Sticker|WD=Q2872553|lt=stickers}} which do not require separate paste. As stickers, they are also placed in {{ill|Book|en|Book|WD=Q571|lt=books}} and on personal items for identification and decoration. A common criticism of the sticker version of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} is that they are more difficult to peel off than their original pasted ancestors, and thus can disfigure the underlying buildings when removed.
==Gallery== <gallery mode="packed" heights="230"> File:Yokai senjafuda.jpg|[[Nōsatsu]], Shōki the Demon Queller File:Senjafuda collector.jpg|Masked {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} collector tossing slips into the air File:Senjafuda foxes.jpg|Fox hairdressers File:Senjafuda fox at gates.jpg|A stone fox statue in front of a series of shrine gates at [[Anamori Inari]], Tokyo File:Senjafuda paster.jpg|Tengu Kōhei with a box for his {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} and pole-mounted brushes used for pasting </gallery>
==See also== * {{transliteration|ja|[[Ofuda]]}}
==Notes== {{Reflist}}
==References== * Gordenker, Alice, "[http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/ek20101118wh.html So, What the Heck is That? Shrine tags]", ''[[:en:Japan Times|Japan Times]]'', 18 November 2010, p. 13.
== External links == * {{Commons category-inline}} * [https://glam.uoregon.edu/yokaisenjafuda/page/world-of-senjafuda World of Senjafuda] University of Oregon * [https://www.miniaturejapanesewoodblocks.net/gallery-6-senjafuda-votive-slips Gallery of senjafuda] Christenson Collection of Miniature Japanese Woodblock Prints

As stickers, they are also placed in {{ill|Book|en|Book|WD=Q571|lt=books}} and on personal items for identification and decoration. A common criticism of the sticker version of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} is that they are more difficult to peel off than their original pasted ancestors, and thus can disfigure the underlying buildings when removed.

== External links == * {{Commons category-inline}} * [https://glam.uoregon.edu/yokaisenjafuda/page/world-of-senjafuda World of Senjafuda] University of Oregon * [https://www.miniaturejapanesewoodblocks.net/gallery-6-senjafuda-votive-slips Gallery of senjafuda] Christenson Collection of Miniature Japanese Woodblock Prints
{{ukiyo-e}} <!--this was an inappropriate and erroneous transclusion meant to be a bottom bar thing I think but not including--> {{Amulets and Talismans}} {{Authority control}}
[[wiktionary:千社札|千社札]]
[[en:Senjafuda]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Senjafuda}} [[fr:Senjafuda]] [[ja:千社札]] [[pl:Senja-fuda]] [[zh:千社札]] {{wikidata link|Q335706}} [[Category:Buddhism in the Edo period]] [[Category:Buddhist religious objects]] [[Category:Culture of Japan]] [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:Shinto religious objects]] [[Category:Ukiyo-e]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Senjafuda]][[en:Senjafuda]][[fr:Senjafuda]][[ja:千社札]][[pl:Senja-fuda]][[zh:千社札]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Senjafuda]][[en:Senjafuda]][[fr:Senjafuda]][[ja:千社札]][[pl:Senja-fuda]][[zh:千社札]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Senjafuda]][[en:Senjafuda]][[fr:Senjafuda]][[ja:千社札]][[pl:Senja-fuda]][[zh:千社札]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q335706]] Wikidata name: Senjafuda [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Handwritten or woodblock-printed pasted to shrine or temple in Japan}} {{Refimprove|date=February 2018}} {{Italic title}}{{Shinto Talismans|image=[[File:八坂神社 (4472701259).jpg|thumb|Examples on a ceiling at [[Yasaka Shrine]]]]}} [[File:Gifu-kegonji0059.JPG|right|thumb|300px|{{transliteration|ja|Senjafuda}} pasted on a shrine gate in {{ill|Gifu|en|Gifu|WD=Q45798|lt=Gifu}}]]
{{nihongo3|{{lit|thousand-shrine tags}}||'''Senjafuda'''}} are [[:en:Votive|Votive]] slips, [[:en:stickers|stickers]] or {{ill|Placard|en|Placard|WD=Q606876|lt=Placard}}s {{ill|Poster|en|Poster|WD=Q429785|lt=posted}} on the gates or buildings of {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}}s and {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|WD=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temples}} in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. Unlike {{transliteration|ja|[[ofuda]]}}, which bear the name of the shrine, {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} bear the name of the worshipper, and can be purchased pre-printed with common names at temples and shrines throughout Japan, as well as at stationery stores and video game centres. {{transliteration|ja|Senjafuda}} were originally made from wooden slats, but have been made of paper since the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}.
A single senjafuda measures 1.6 {{transliteration|ja|{{ill|Cun (unit)|en|Cun (unit)|WD=Q876237|lt=sun}}}} ({{cvt|58|mm}}) in width and 4.8 {{transl|ja|sun}} ({{cvt|173|mm}}) in height. This gives the {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} a ratio of 1:3. A frame is drawn inside this space which contains the lettering or pictures. In 1887, a measurement for this frame was also established as {{cvt|48|mm}} wide and {{cvt|144|mm}} tall.
Ordinarily, the designs were used to commemorate a visit to a temple or shrine and printed with simple monochromatic schemes, but eventually aesthetic sense gave way to colorful variations and designs. In the pleasure quarters of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}, colorful designs were employed on {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} and used in place of traditional business cards. This variation is called {{transliteration|ja|hana-meishi}} which roughly translated to "flower business card." Today, the "business card" use of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} is the most common.
{{transliteration|ja|Senjafuda}} were primarily printed with {{transliteration|ja|{{ill|Edomoji|en|Edomoji|WD=Q1133518|lt=Edomoji}}}}, or Edo-period lettering styles, and pressed with the same traditional wooden boards used to produce [[ukiyo-e]] prints. Stickers on shrines are often pasted in very obvious, easily seen locations, but a variation on this practice is to purposely obscure the location of the {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} in order to protect it from exposure to wind and rain and thus prolong its presence.
== History == {{transliteration|ja|Senjafuda}} were first produced in the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}} (794–1185) when shrine worshipers made pilgrimages to visits to many shrines and worship the Buddhist goddess of mercy, [[Kannon]]. They were not originally made of paper, they were first made from wooden slats that were hung from the gates of Kannon temples by nails made of {{ill|Bamboo|en|Bamboo|WD=Q670887|lt=Bamboo}}. The slats were carved out with the visitors' name, area of origin and often included a prayer for a good life and {{ill|Afterlife|en|Afterlife|WD=Q623282|lt=Afterlife}}.
There are two styles of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}}: the older style, {{transliteration|ja|daimei nosatsu}}, and the newer style, {{transliteration|ja|kokan nosatsu}}. {{transliteration|ja|Daimei nosatsu}} are basic black ink on white paper. The {{ill|Inkstick|en|Inkstick|WD=Q11240146|lt=ink}} used is so strong that after the printed {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} are placed on the [[Torii|shrine or temple gate]], years later when the paper is peeled away, the ink remains. Therefore, many shrine {{transliteration|ja|[[kannushi]]}} or {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Shinshoku|Shinshoku]]}} do not like the use of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}}, as well as more modern practices, where younger {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} practitioners do not pray or buy a stamp from the shrine before applying their {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}}.
[[File:Hiroshige senjafuda.jpg|thumb|upright=1.3|[[Hiroshige]] {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} depicting an exchange meeting in 1842. One of the hanging banners ironically states "Graffiti strictly forbidden".]] The later style of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} are called {{transliteration|ja|kokan nosatsu}} and originated in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} (1603–1868). During the beginning of the Edo period, shrine pilgrimages gained popularity, beginning the tradition known as {{transliteration|ja|[[senjamairi]]}}, meaning "a thousand shrine visits for good luck". {{transliteration|ja|Kokan nosatsu senjafuda}} are a lot more colorful than {{transliteration|ja|saimei nosatsu}}, and have rich patterns and designs, being used more as novelty items and more like trading cards or the business cards of today. Like many things during the Edo period, {{transliteration|ja|kokan nosatsu senjafuda}} were regulated, with the number of colours on a person's {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} limited to their class and place in society.
Because of this, collectors who enjoyed the many designs and colors of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} began meeting to exchange them with one another;<ref name="UofOregon">{{cite web | title=The world of senjafuda | website=Mellon Projects | url=https://glam.uoregon.edu/s/yokai-senjafuda/page/world-of-senjafuda | access-date=24 November 2022}}</ref> first, the meetings took place at private homes, and then later were arranged for public places like restaurants and expensive {{ill|Ochaya|en|Ochaya|WD=Q7076300|lt=tea houses}}. According to Kiritani's ''Vanishing Japan'', the oldest surviving invitation card to a {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} meeting dates back to 1799.<ref>Kiritani, Elizabeth ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=5_GGbiw1-y4C&dq=vanishing+japan&pg=PA1 Vanishing Japan]''</ref> Due to the growing popularity of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} meetings, the [[Government of Japan|Japanese government]] enforced a law forbidding their trading, which did not stop the meetings from taking place. {{transliteration|ja|Senjafuda}} meetings continue to this day, with collectors and aficionados alike meeting to share and trade their own designs as well as admire others.
US collector and Japanese anthropologist {{ill|Frederick Starr|en|Frederick Starr|WD=Q5498791|lt=Frederick Starr}} was a turn-of-the-century collector and avid participant in {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} or {{transliteration|ja|nōsatsu-kai}} (votive slip exchange clubs), so much so that he was given the name "Dr. Ofuda". He collected tens of thousands of slips, and a fellow collector and popular art enthusiast, {{ill|Gertrude Bass Warner|en|Gertrude Bass Warner|WD=Q54867731|lt=Gertrude Bass Warner}}, purchased much of his collection. It currently resides at the {{ill|University of Oregon|en|University of Oregon|WD=Q766145|lt=University of Oregon}} Knight Library Special Collections & University Archives, part of the Gertrude Bass Warner Collection, and examples are viewable online at UO Oregon Digital.<ref>{{cite web|last1=McDowell|first1=Kevin|title=Gertrude Bass Warner Collection of Japanese Shrine and Temple Votive Slips (nōsatsu)|url=https://oregondigital.org/sets/gb-warner-nosatsu|website=Oregon Digital|publisher=University of Oregon|access-date=3 May 2017}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|last1=McDowell|first1=Kevin|title=Rare Collection: Nōsatsu Japanese Shrine and Temple Votive Slips|url=https://blogs.uoregon.edu/scua/2016/05/09/rare-collection-nosatsu-japanese-shrine-and-temple-votive-slips/|website=Upbound UO Blog|publisher=Special Collections & University Archives, University of Oregon|access-date=3 May 2017}}</ref>
== Construction == {{transliteration|ja|Senjafuda}} used to be made from {{ill|Rice paper|en|Rice paper|WD=Q1066536|lt=Rice paper}} with ink called {{transliteration|ja|sumi}}, and were pasted on with a starchy rice paste. The pilgrims used to carry walking staffs for their long journeys, which doubled as an applicator for {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}}. The paste was applied with something called {{transliteration|ja|meotobake}} – two brushes about 30 degrees apart, with a clip on the other side of the brushes, allowing {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} to be pasted in out of reach areas, leaving others to wonder exactly how they got up there.
In the present day, {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} are made from printed paper, and are rarely made traditionally through wood block printing. Wooden slat {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}}, however, are still produced, and are worn as a {{ill|Necklace|en|Necklace|WD=Q189299|lt=Necklace}} or used for {{ill|keychain|en|keychain|WD=Q51338|lt=key chain}} and {{ill|Mobile phone accessories|en|Mobile phone accessories|WD=Q6887188|lt=cell phone ornaments}}. The ones made from paper are pre-printed with common names; machines are also available that can produce custom {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} with adhesive backings.
== Famous figures == Some famous producers of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} are Hiroshige, Eisen, Kunisada, Kuniyoshi. They mainly produced {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}}, due to the expensive of the ukiyo-e printing process.
Senrei Sekioka was one of the foremost Japanese experts of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} history; Iseman and Frederick Starr were also important members of the {{transliteration|ja|nosatsu-kai}} during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji}} and {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō}} eras.
== Modern-day {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} == {{transliteration|ja|Senjafuda}} are also sold as {{ill|Sticker|en|Sticker|WD=Q2872553|lt=stickers}} which do not require separate paste. As stickers, they are also placed in {{ill|Book|en|Book|WD=Q571|lt=books}} and on personal items for identification and decoration. A common criticism of the sticker version of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} is that they are more difficult to peel off than their original pasted ancestors, and thus can disfigure the underlying buildings when removed.
==Gallery== <gallery mode="packed" heights="230"> File:Yokai senjafuda.jpg|[[Nōsatsu]], Shōki the Demon Queller File:Senjafuda collector.jpg|Masked {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} collector tossing slips into the air File:Senjafuda foxes.jpg|Fox hairdressers File:Senjafuda fox at gates.jpg|A stone fox statue in front of a series of shrine gates at [[Anamori Inari]], Tokyo File:Senjafuda paster.jpg|Tengu Kōhei with a box for his {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} and pole-mounted brushes used for pasting </gallery>

A common criticism of the sticker version of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} is that they are more difficult to peel off than their original pasted ancestors, and thus can disfigure the underlying buildings when removed.

==See also== * {{transliteration|ja|[[Ofuda]]}}

[[wiktionary:千社札|千社札]]
[[en:Senjafuda]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Senjafuda}} [[fr:Senjafuda]] [[ja:千社札]] [[pl:Senja-fuda]] [[zh:千社札]] {{wikidata link|Q335706}} [[Category:Buddhism in the Edo period]] [[Category:Buddhist religious objects]] [[Category:Culture of Japan]] [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:Shinto religious objects]] [[Category:Ukiyo-e]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Senjafuda]][[en:Senjafuda]][[fr:Senjafuda]][[ja:千社札]][[pl:Senja-fuda]][[zh:千社札]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Senjafuda]][[en:Senjafuda]][[fr:Senjafuda]][[ja:千社札]][[pl:Senja-fuda]][[zh:千社札]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Senjafuda]][[en:Senjafuda]][[fr:Senjafuda]][[ja:千社札]][[pl:Senja-fuda]][[zh:千社札]]
{{Short description|Handwritten or woodblock-printed pasted to shrine or temple in Japan}} {{Refimprove|date=February 2018}} {{Italic title}}{{Shinto Talismans|image=[[File:八坂神社 (4472701259).jpg|thumb|Examples on a ceiling at [[Yasaka Shrine]]]]}} [[File:Gifu-kegonji0059.JPG|right|thumb|300px|{{transliteration|ja|Senjafuda}} pasted on a shrine gate in {{ill|Gifu|en|Gifu|WD=Q45798|lt=Gifu}}]]
{{nihongo3|{{lit|thousand-shrine tags}}||'''Senjafuda'''}} are [[:en:Votive|Votive]] slips, [[:en:stickers|stickers]] or {{ill|Placard|en|Placard|WD=Q606876|lt=Placard}}s {{ill|Poster|en|Poster|WD=Q429785|lt=posted}} on the gates or buildings of {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}}s and {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|WD=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temples}} in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. Unlike {{transliteration|ja|[[ofuda]]}}, which bear the name of the shrine, {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} bear the name of the worshipper, and can be purchased pre-printed with common names at temples and shrines throughout Japan, as well as at stationery stores and video game centres. {{transliteration|ja|Senjafuda}} were originally made from wooden slats, but have been made of paper since the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}.
A single senjafuda measures 1.6 {{transliteration|ja|{{ill|Cun (unit)|en|Cun (unit)|WD=Q876237|lt=sun}}}} ({{cvt|58|mm}}) in width and 4.8 {{transl|ja|sun}} ({{cvt|173|mm}}) in height. This gives the {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} a ratio of 1:3. A frame is drawn inside this space which contains the lettering or pictures. In 1887, a measurement for this frame was also established as {{cvt|48|mm}} wide and {{cvt|144|mm}} tall.
Ordinarily, the designs were used to commemorate a visit to a temple or shrine and printed with simple monochromatic schemes, but eventually aesthetic sense gave way to colorful variations and designs. In the pleasure quarters of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}, colorful designs were employed on {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} and used in place of traditional business cards. This variation is called {{transliteration|ja|hana-meishi}} which roughly translated to "flower business card." Today, the "business card" use of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} is the most common.
{{transliteration|ja|Senjafuda}} were primarily printed with {{transliteration|ja|{{ill|Edomoji|en|Edomoji|WD=Q1133518|lt=Edomoji}}}}, or Edo-period lettering styles, and pressed with the same traditional wooden boards used to produce [[ukiyo-e]] prints. Stickers on shrines are often pasted in very obvious, easily seen locations, but a variation on this practice is to purposely obscure the location of the {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} in order to protect it from exposure to wind and rain and thus prolong its presence.
== History == {{transliteration|ja|Senjafuda}} were first produced in the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}} (794–1185) when shrine worshipers made pilgrimages to visits to many shrines and worship the Buddhist goddess of mercy, [[Kannon]]. They were not originally made of paper, they were first made from wooden slats that were hung from the gates of Kannon temples by nails made of {{ill|Bamboo|en|Bamboo|WD=Q670887|lt=Bamboo}}. The slats were carved out with the visitors' name, area of origin and often included a prayer for a good life and {{ill|Afterlife|en|Afterlife|WD=Q623282|lt=Afterlife}}.
There are two styles of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}}: the older style, {{transliteration|ja|daimei nosatsu}}, and the newer style, {{transliteration|ja|kokan nosatsu}}. {{transliteration|ja|Daimei nosatsu}} are basic black ink on white paper. The {{ill|Inkstick|en|Inkstick|WD=Q11240146|lt=ink}} used is so strong that after the printed {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} are placed on the [[Torii|shrine or temple gate]], years later when the paper is peeled away, the ink remains. Therefore, many shrine {{transliteration|ja|[[kannushi]]}} or {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Shinshoku|Shinshoku]]}} do not like the use of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}}, as well as more modern practices, where younger {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} practitioners do not pray or buy a stamp from the shrine before applying their {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}}.
[[File:Hiroshige senjafuda.jpg|thumb|upright=1.3|[[Hiroshige]] {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} depicting an exchange meeting in 1842. One of the hanging banners ironically states "Graffiti strictly forbidden".]] The later style of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} are called {{transliteration|ja|kokan nosatsu}} and originated in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} (1603–1868). During the beginning of the Edo period, shrine pilgrimages gained popularity, beginning the tradition known as {{transliteration|ja|[[senjamairi]]}}, meaning "a thousand shrine visits for good luck". {{transliteration|ja|Kokan nosatsu senjafuda}} are a lot more colorful than {{transliteration|ja|saimei nosatsu}}, and have rich patterns and designs, being used more as novelty items and more like trading cards or the business cards of today. Like many things during the Edo period, {{transliteration|ja|kokan nosatsu senjafuda}} were regulated, with the number of colours on a person's {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} limited to their class and place in society.
Because of this, collectors who enjoyed the many designs and colors of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} began meeting to exchange them with one another;<ref name="UofOregon">{{cite web | title=The world of senjafuda | website=Mellon Projects | url=https://glam.uoregon.edu/s/yokai-senjafuda/page/world-of-senjafuda | access-date=24 November 2022}}</ref> first, the meetings took place at private homes, and then later were arranged for public places like restaurants and expensive {{ill|Ochaya|en|Ochaya|WD=Q7076300|lt=tea houses}}. According to Kiritani's ''Vanishing Japan'', the oldest surviving invitation card to a {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} meeting dates back to 1799.<ref>Kiritani, Elizabeth ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=5_GGbiw1-y4C&dq=vanishing+japan&pg=PA1 Vanishing Japan]''</ref> Due to the growing popularity of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} meetings, the [[Government of Japan|Japanese government]] enforced a law forbidding their trading, which did not stop the meetings from taking place. {{transliteration|ja|Senjafuda}} meetings continue to this day, with collectors and aficionados alike meeting to share and trade their own designs as well as admire others.
US collector and Japanese anthropologist {{ill|Frederick Starr|en|Frederick Starr|WD=Q5498791|lt=Frederick Starr}} was a turn-of-the-century collector and avid participant in {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} or {{transliteration|ja|nōsatsu-kai}} (votive slip exchange clubs), so much so that he was given the name "Dr. Ofuda". He collected tens of thousands of slips, and a fellow collector and popular art enthusiast, {{ill|Gertrude Bass Warner|en|Gertrude Bass Warner|WD=Q54867731|lt=Gertrude Bass Warner}}, purchased much of his collection. It currently resides at the {{ill|University of Oregon|en|University of Oregon|WD=Q766145|lt=University of Oregon}} Knight Library Special Collections & University Archives, part of the Gertrude Bass Warner Collection, and examples are viewable online at UO Oregon Digital.<ref>{{cite web|last1=McDowell|first1=Kevin|title=Gertrude Bass Warner Collection of Japanese Shrine and Temple Votive Slips (nōsatsu)|url=https://oregondigital.org/sets/gb-warner-nosatsu|website=Oregon Digital|publisher=University of Oregon|access-date=3 May 2017}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|last1=McDowell|first1=Kevin|title=Rare Collection: Nōsatsu Japanese Shrine and Temple Votive Slips|url=https://blogs.uoregon.edu/scua/2016/05/09/rare-collection-nosatsu-japanese-shrine-and-temple-votive-slips/|website=Upbound UO Blog|publisher=Special Collections & University Archives, University of Oregon|access-date=3 May 2017}}</ref>
== Construction == {{transliteration|ja|Senjafuda}} used to be made from {{ill|Rice paper|en|Rice paper|WD=Q1066536|lt=Rice paper}} with ink called {{transliteration|ja|sumi}}, and were pasted on with a starchy rice paste. The pilgrims used to carry walking staffs for their long journeys, which doubled as an applicator for {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}}. The paste was applied with something called {{transliteration|ja|meotobake}} – two brushes about 30 degrees apart, with a clip on the other side of the brushes, allowing {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} to be pasted in out of reach areas, leaving others to wonder exactly how they got up there.
In the present day, {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} are made from printed paper, and are rarely made traditionally through wood block printing. Wooden slat {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}}, however, are still produced, and are worn as a {{ill|Necklace|en|Necklace|WD=Q189299|lt=Necklace}} or used for {{ill|keychain|en|keychain|WD=Q51338|lt=key chain}} and {{ill|Mobile phone accessories|en|Mobile phone accessories|WD=Q6887188|lt=cell phone ornaments}}. The ones made from paper are pre-printed with common names; machines are also available that can produce custom {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} with adhesive backings.
== Famous figures == Some famous producers of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} are Hiroshige, Eisen, Kunisada, Kuniyoshi. They mainly produced {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}}, due to the expensive of the ukiyo-e printing process.
Senrei Sekioka was one of the foremost Japanese experts of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} history; Iseman and Frederick Starr were also important members of the {{transliteration|ja|nosatsu-kai}} during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji}} and {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō}} eras.
== Modern-day {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} == {{transliteration|ja|Senjafuda}} are also sold as {{ill|Sticker|en|Sticker|WD=Q2872553|lt=stickers}} which do not require separate paste. As stickers, they are also placed in {{ill|Book|en|Book|WD=Q571|lt=books}} and on personal items for identification and decoration. A common criticism of the sticker version of {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} is that they are more difficult to peel off than their original pasted ancestors, and thus can disfigure the underlying buildings when removed.
==Gallery== <gallery mode="packed" heights="230"> File:Yokai senjafuda.jpg|[[Nōsatsu]], Shōki the Demon Queller File:Senjafuda collector.jpg|Masked {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} collector tossing slips into the air File:Senjafuda foxes.jpg|Fox hairdressers File:Senjafuda fox at gates.jpg|A stone fox statue in front of a series of shrine gates at [[Anamori Inari]], Tokyo File:Senjafuda paster.jpg|Tengu Kōhei with a box for his {{transliteration|ja|senjafuda}} and pole-mounted brushes used for pasting </gallery>
==See also== * {{transliteration|ja|[[Ofuda]]}}
==Notes== {{Reflist}}
==References== * Gordenker, Alice, "[http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/ek20101118wh.html So, What the Heck is That? Shrine tags]", ''[[:en:Japan Times|Japan Times]]'', 18 November 2010, p. 13.
== External links == * {{Commons category-inline}} * [https://glam.uoregon.edu/yokaisenjafuda/page/world-of-senjafuda World of Senjafuda] University of Oregon * [https://www.miniaturejapanesewoodblocks.net/gallery-6-senjafuda-votive-slips Gallery of senjafuda] Christenson Collection of Miniature Japanese Woodblock Prints
